# faveoff — Initial Discussion

_Generated by the autonomous multi-agent orchestrator on 2026-07-08 12:19:01._

## Original Prompt

```
PORTFOLIO_CHILD_PROJECT: true
Parent portfolio: multi-app-exp7
Selected app slug: faveoff

Build this app as a working local-first SwiftUI MVP.

## Selected App Spec

# FaveOff

Build mode: **MVP build**.
Build priority: **4**.

## App Name

FaveOff

## Category

Entertainment / Taste Ranking

## One-Sentence Promise

Rank the movies and shows you've watched head-to-head, and FaveOff builds your real, personal top 10 — then turns it into a shareable taste card.

## Target User

Anyone who watches movies/shows and has opinions but finds star ratings meaningless and social-network movie apps like overhead — mass-market watchers, not cinephile completionists.

## Painful Moment Solved

Rating a movie 1-5 stars and realizing that number means nothing next to the 40 other movies you've also rated 4 stars — you genuinely don't know your own top 10.

## Why It Is Different

Instead of star ratings, shows two watched titles and asks which was better, building an Elo-style ranked list from a handful of quick head-to-head picks — far more decisive than star ratings, ending in a poster-grid 'Taste Card' built for sharing, with zero social graph or account required to get value.

## Why Users Would Pay

Free tier ranks movies only with a capped list length; FaveOff+ unlocks TV shows and music/albums as separate ranking tracks, unlimited list length, genre-filtered sub-rankings, and periodic 'Wrapped'-style recap cards.

## Subscription Model

Freemium, $3.99/mo or $24.99/yr.

## Local-First MVP Scope

SwiftData store for titles, comparisons, and computed rankings; ranking algorithm runs fully on-device; title/poster metadata lookup is the one network dependency, isolated behind a protocol.

## Future Backend Roadmap

Repository boundary allows CloudKit sync across devices and, later, an optional social taste-comparison layer without touching the core ranking engine.

## AR / Local ML / Local LLM Fit

None load-bearing; the hard technical capability is a proper Elo/merge-sort-style pairwise ranking engine, not ML.

## Design Direction

Cinematic dark-mode-first system — near-black backgrounds, marquee-red accent, bold condensed poster-style typography, poster artwork as the dominant visual element, film-reel-inspired transitions.

## App Store Positioning

Star ratings lie to you. FaveOff makes you pick, one match-up at a time, until your real top 10 falls out.

## Invalidation Criteria

If users abandon mid-ranking after only a handful of comparisons and never reach a shareable top-10, the comparison flow is too tedious and needs a faster convergence algorithm or smaller default list size.

## v1 Build Scope

Add titles (movies+TV), head-to-head ranking engine, ranked list view, Taste Card generation + ShareLink, FaveOff+ paywall with StoreKit.

## Core Workflows

- add a watched title
- head-to-head comparison rounds
- browse computed ranked list
- generate & share Taste Card (poster grid of top 10)
- filter rankings by genre/decade

## iOS-Native Features

- ShareLink for Taste Card
- ImageRenderer for poster-grid card generation
- WidgetKit small widget showing current #1

## Key Risks

- The one network touchpoint (title/poster lookup) must degrade gracefully offline (initials/color-block placeholder), never blocking the core ranking loop
- Comparison fatigue on large libraries — needs a smart pairing algorithm scaling ~n log n, not full round-robin n²

## Claude Design Handoff Prompt

Design a cinematic entertainment-ranking app called FaveOff: near-black dark-mode-first backgrounds, marquee-red accent, bold condensed poster-style typography, movie/show poster art as the dominant visual content, film-reel-inspired swipe/transition motion for head-to-head comparison screens, feels like a red-carpet awards show, not a spreadsheet.

## Parent Portfolio Prompt

Build 5 completely separate, production-ready iOS apps at the same time.

This is a multi-app PORTFOLIO request: multiple apps in one program. Each selected app becomes its own separate project — one folder per app, never bundled together.

Pick any 5 categories/concepts — no fixed assignment. Choose the 5 strongest MASS-MARKET ideas.

TOP PRIORITY — VIRAL, MASS-MARKET APPEAL (hard requirement):
- Every app must target a BROAD, mainstream audience. NO niche-community apps (no hobbyist tools, no professional verticals, no special-interest utilities). If your parents, your barista, and a college student wouldn't all instantly get it, it fails.
- Each app must have a built-in viral loop: something people naturally share, screenshot, send to friends, or compete over (shareable results/cards, streaks, challenges, comparisons, "send this to a friend" moments).
- The concept must be explainable in one sentence and demo-able in 10 seconds.
- Think the scale of habit trackers, photo tools, social utilities, everyday life tools, casual self-improvement, money, food, sleep, relationships, entertainment — things with tens of millions of potential users.

SECOND PRIORITY — DESIGN & UI/UX:
- World-class, distinctive visual identity per app: deliberate color system, type scale, spacing, iconography, motion language — not stock SwiftUI defaults.
- "Apple Design Award" caliber polish. Every screen considered: empty, loading, error states, transitions, haptics, spring animations.
- Dark mode, Dynamic Type, accessibility (VoiceOver, 44pt targets, WCAG AA contrast).
- Each of the 5 apps must look clearly DIFFERENT from the other four — five distinct design directions.
- Documented design system per app (tokens, components, states); built UI must match it.

THIRD PRIORITY — COMPLEXITY & DEPTH:
- Genuinely substantial apps — interconnected features, real domain data model, non-trivial logic, offline persistence with sync-later architecture, robust settings, import/export, and at least one hard technical capability done well.
- Depth that rewards daily use — but never at the expense of instant mass-market clarity.

FOURTH PRIORITY — MONETIZATION:
- Clear, sustainable revenue path per app (subscription tiers, IAP, premium) that feels native to the product, never punitive. Free tier hooks users; paid tier is compelling.

General Requirements:
1. Each app goes from 0 to production-ready.
2. Each app must be unique, useful, and commercially viable, better than competitors in a meaningful way.
3. Local-first, architected so cloud support can be added later without rewriting.

UNIQUENESS (hard rule, non-negotiable):
- No app in this batch may be similar to another app in this batch.
- No app may be similar to anything already built in this workspace: Waylay, TrueScale, Provenance, Scope-Guard, Practice-Loop, Proof, ReturnWise, VerveCoach, CueKeeper, brinekeeper, chalkline, quietpilot, cistern, thicket, undersong, undertow, heddle, marginalia, skyglass, and the ~50 apps of prior batches (aarti-path, afterglow, backtimer, chapterly, cleanline, clearpath-access, crockwatch, crosswire, crux-cycle, curio, dayframe, dharma-trail, docket, flinch, heartline, kitbag, night-sky-logbook, pattern-pulse, pillcheck, referral-loop, risk-compass, rollcall, runway, scenario-ledger, shelf, sincerely, tabzero, talkthrough, tessella, throughline, trestle, triptally, umbra, wordsmith-atlas, and others).
- Produce differentiated concepts even if a category overlaps.

Bonus points (optional, only when integral): unique use of LLMs, AR, or ML.

Build rules:
- Run the five app efforts in parallel; keep phases separate per app.
- TIME MATTERS on this batch: reach consensus quickly in discussion phases (end phases early on agreement), but never skip the build, and never mark an app done unless a real, compiling Xcode project with full source exists in app_build. An app without a buildable Xcode project is a FAILURE.
- Save all phase discussions; combine each app's transcript into a .txt at the end.
- Recover cleanly from stalls; report only important milestones.

Output:
- One folder per app in the output directory — never one folder containing all apps.
Output directory:
- /Users/pchordia/Documents/iOS-App-Factory

Now generate at least 10 candidate ideas, judge them primarily on mass-market viral potential, choose the best 5, and build all 5 to production quality with exceptional UI/UX.
```

## Phase Purpose

Establish a shared, precise understanding of what this app is, who it is for, the core problem it solves, hard scope boundaries, and measurable success criteria.

## Transcript


### Round 1


**Codex (Product Strategist · User Advocate) — Round 1**

You should lock FaveOff to this one promise: turn two watched titles into a reliable personal top ten with fast head-to-head picks, then make that ranking instantly shareable as a Taste Card.  
The primary user is the casual film/TV watcher who has opinions but not a structured system; imagine a Friday-night friend asking “what should we watch this weekend?” and this person wants to produce a confident, defensible top list in minutes without a spreadsheet.  
The core loop is: add a watched title (manual by default), compare it against one existing title in the ranked list via binary-insertion pairings, immediately place it, and keep rankable items surfaced as a living top list that can be filtered and rendered into a card.  
No accounts, no social graph, no network-first backend, no music-album ranking in v1, no ML model in the loop, and no full round-robin matching—everything is local-first with SwiftData-backed titles/comparisons/rank state and merge/binary-style bounded ranking.  
The most important ambiguity to resolve now is that music/albums is v2; this phase ships movies as free-track plus TV entries stored but only ranked in paid track to honor the spec without killing v1 utility.  
The biggest risk is trust collapse from awkward or arbitrary comparisons, so ranking must stay bounded, incremental, and transparent enough that users feel “this got better with each tap” rather than trapped in random admin tasks.  
Success has to be measurable: first-time users should reach a populated shareable top-10 in no more than ~10 added titles and under 5 minutes, with >85% of added titles ranked automatically, no crashes or blocked flows without network, successful local persistence across relaunch, at least one accessible comparison path (VoiceOver + Dynamic Type), and a working offline path where placeholder art still renders and sharing/export still works.  
That last constraint must be preserved in every build decision: if adding anything makes first-run too instruction-heavy or blocks the free loop, it’s out.

**Claude (Design Lead · Systems Thinker) — Round 1**

Let me give my independent read on this, thinking about it as the actual screens and flows someone will tap through, not just the pitch.

The product promise I'd commit to: FaveOff turns a handful of "which was better" taps into a real, trustworthy top 10 of the movies you've watched, and hands you back something worth sending to a group chat. If I had to say it to someone at a party in one breath: it's the app that makes you pick, so you finally know your own favorite movies.

Primary user and scenario: someone who just finished watching something, or is scrolling a few days later, and thinks "actually, was that better than [other thing]?" — not a completionist logging every viewing, not someone building an archive. They open the app maybe once a week, add 2-4 things they watched recently, get shown a few quick match-ups, and check where their list stands. The scenario that matters most for scope isn't "onboarding with 20 titles at once" — it's the steady-state loop of "I watched one thing, let me place it," because that's what happens 500 times over the life of the app versus once at install.

Core loop, concretely as screens: Add (type a title, pick from local suggestions or fall back to manual entry with a color-block poster) → Compare (one or a small bounded number of head-to-head screens where you tap the better one, or say "skip/haven't thought about it") → Placed (the new title drops into the ranked list at its resolved position, with a short "here's where it landed" beat — this confirmation moment is the payoff and I don't think the spec history has fully named it as its own screen state) → Browse (scrollable ranked list, filterable) → Share (Taste Card render + ShareLink). The thing I want to flag now, before anyone locks a wireframe: "Placed" needs to exist as a distinct, small celebratory state — even just a card that says "#4 of 12, ahead of X, behind Y" — because without a visible payoff after 2-4 comparisons, the loop is just work with no reward, and that's exactly the abandonment mode the invalidation criteria is worried about. If we skip straight from the last comparison tap back into the plain list view with no acknowledgment, we've built the boring version of this app.

Hard scope boundaries I'd defend: v1 is movies-and-TV-addable but movies-only-ranked-for-free (already decided), one taste-ranking track surfaced at a time in the UI (no split-screen "compare across categories"), no editing/reordering the list by hand — the whole pitch is that you don't get to fudge your own ranking, the comparisons are the only lever, and letting people drag-reorder would undercut the "this is your real taste, not your curated image" promise in the App Store positioning. No notes/reviews/tags beyond what's needed for genre/decade filtering. No onboarding tutorial screens that require reading — if the comparison screen itself doesn't make the two-buttons-pick-one interaction self-evident, that's a design failure to fix, not something to patch with an explainer.

Where I think there's real ambiguity nobody's resolved yet: what happens when someone re-opens the app with zero pending comparisons because their whole library is fully placed? Is there a "re-litigate an old match-up" affordance, or does the app just sit there showing the list with an "add something to keep ranking" prompt? I'd argue for the latter — no forced re-comparison, because manufacturing artificial comparisons to keep engagement up cuts against the "your real opinion" premise and risks feeling gamed. The empty/steady-state screen when there's nothing to compare needs to be a first-class state, not an afterthought, because for an active user this might be the most common thing they see.

Measurable success criteria I'd hold this to: a new user with zero titles can reach a shareable card (even a 3-5 item one, doesn't need to be a full 10) in under 3 minutes of actual interaction time; the ranking after N comparisons should be stable — re-showing the same two titles shouldn't ever produce a contradictory forced order; and the specific abandonment signal called out in the invalidation criteria (quits after "a handful" of comparisons, never reaches a shareable list) needs an actual operational definition before we can call the MVP validated — I'd propose: if a majority of first-session users don't complete at least 5 comparisons, that's the tedious-flow failure state, and the fix path is smaller default list size / faster convergence, not more onboarding polish.

The biggest risk to my own framing: I'm pushing hard against any manual reordering or tutorial screens in the name of "purity of the mechanic," and that's a bet that the comparison screen's affordances are strong enough to be self-teaching. If playtesting (or even just a critical look at the wireframe) shows people genuinely don't know what to do on first launch, "no tutorial" becomes a stubborn ideology rather than good design, and I'd rather we name that tradeoff now than discover it after the screens are built.

**Coordinator (Claude) — decision after round 1**

CONSENSUS: YES

## Final Output

Codex and Claude are lined up here — same promise, same user, same loop, and Claude's additions fill in gaps rather than fight anything Codex said.

**Product promise:** FaveOff turns quick head-to-head picks between watched titles into a real, trustworthy personal top 10 — something you'd actually be confident sharing, not a star rating you don't believe.

**Primary user & scenario:** A casual movie/TV watcher who has opinions but no system for them — not a completionist, not someone building an archive. The scenario that matters most isn't a big one-time onboarding dump; it's the steady-state habit of "I watched something, let me place it" — add a couple of titles a week, get a couple of quick comparisons, watch the list update. That recurring small loop is what the app needs to nail, not a one-time gauntlet.

**Core loop:** Add a title (manual entry by default, offline-safe placeholder art) → Compare it against the ranked list via bounded binary-insertion match-ups (with a skip/"haven't thought about it" escape) → **Placed** — a distinct, small payoff moment showing where it landed ("#4 of 12, ahead of X, behind Y") that both agree is the actual reward beat and can't be skipped over silently → Browse the living ranked list, filterable by genre/decade → Share a Taste Card via ImageRenderer + ShareLink. When there's nothing left to compare, the app shows a calm steady-state ("add something to keep ranking") rather than manufacturing forced re-comparisons just to stay engaging.

**Hard scope boundaries:** No accounts, no social graph, no network-first architecture, no music/album track in v1, no ML in the ranking loop, no full round-robin (n²) comparisons. Movies are the free-ranked track; TV titles can be added/logged for free but only ranked after upgrading (carried over from earlier phases). No manual drag-to-reorder of the list — the whole point is that comparisons, not curation, produce the order. No tutorial/instruction screens — if the comparison screen's two-tap interaction isn't self-evident on its own, that's a design defect to fix, not something to patch with an explainer.

**Measurable success criteria:** A brand-new user should reach a shareable card (even a small one, 3–5 titles) in a few minutes of real interaction, not a big session; ranking must be stable — the same two titles should never resolve to a contradictory order later; and the spec's own "abandons after a handful of comparisons" failure mode gets an operational definition: if most first-session users don't complete at least 5 comparisons, that's the tedious-flow failure, and the fix is a smaller default list or faster convergence — not more onboarding. Offline behavior (placeholder art, working share/export with no network) and persistence across relaunch are part of the bar, not stretch goals.

**Open tension flagged, not a blocker:** Claude names its own risk here — betting that "no tutorial, no manual reorder" holds up once real screens exist. If a later phase's wireframes show people genuinely lost on first launch, that's worth revisiting rather than defending on principle.

**Quality Gate (Claude) — after round 1**

QUALITY: PASS

## Feedback
This output meets the phase bar and can close. All five required elements are present and concrete: a one-sentence product promise, a clearly scoped primary user/scenario (steady-state "I watched something, let me place it" habit rather than a one-time onboarding dump), a core loop that names a genuinely new, load-bearing detail (the "Placed" payoff state as a distinct screen, not implied) without contradicting anything decided in `prompt_contract` or `product_research`, explicit hard scope boundaries (no accounts/social/network-first/music-track/ML/round-robin, plus two new defensible boundaries — no manual reorder, no tutorial screens — that are consistent with the app's core "your real taste, not curated" premise), and measurable success criteria with an operational definition of the spec's own invalidation criterion (≥5 comparisons in first session as the tedious-flow threshold).

It correctly carries forward prior-phase decisions (movies-free/TV-paid track gate, binary-insertion with skip escape, offline-safe defaults) without re-litigating them, and it surfaces the round's real tension (Claude's "no tutorial/no reorder" bet) as an explicit open risk rather than suppressing it — satisfying the instruction to incorporate disagreements rather than paper over them. A developer could explain this app in one sentence, the scope is tight enough for one build pass, and non-goals are unambiguous. No repair needed.

PER APP PRODUCT BRIEF (PER_APP_PRODUCT_BRIEF)

# faveoff — Per App Product Brief

_Generated by the autonomous multi-agent orchestrator on 2026-07-08 12:20:17._

## Original Prompt

```
PORTFOLIO_CHILD_PROJECT: true
Parent portfolio: multi-app-exp7
Selected app slug: faveoff

Build this app as a working local-first SwiftUI MVP.

## Selected App Spec

# FaveOff

Build mode: **MVP build**.
Build priority: **4**.

## App Name

FaveOff

## Category

Entertainment / Taste Ranking

## One-Sentence Promise

Rank the movies and shows you've watched head-to-head, and FaveOff builds your real, personal top 10 — then turns it into a shareable taste card.

## Target User

Anyone who watches movies/shows and has opinions but finds star ratings meaningless and social-network movie apps like overhead — mass-market watchers, not cinephile completionists.

## Painful Moment Solved

Rating a movie 1-5 stars and realizing that number means nothing next to the 40 other movies you've also rated 4 stars — you genuinely don't know your own top 10.

## Why It Is Different

Instead of star ratings, shows two watched titles and asks which was better, building an Elo-style ranked list from a handful of quick head-to-head picks — far more decisive than star ratings, ending in a poster-grid 'Taste Card' built for sharing, with zero social graph or account required to get value.

## Why Users Would Pay

Free tier ranks movies only with a capped list length; FaveOff+ unlocks TV shows and music/albums as separate ranking tracks, unlimited list length, genre-filtered sub-rankings, and periodic 'Wrapped'-style recap cards.

## Subscription Model

Freemium, $3.99/mo or $24.99/yr.

## Local-First MVP Scope

SwiftData store for titles, comparisons, and computed rankings; ranking algorithm runs fully on-device; title/poster metadata lookup is the one network dependency, isolated behind a protocol.

## Future Backend Roadmap

Repository boundary allows CloudKit sync across devices and, later, an optional social taste-comparison layer without touching the core ranking engine.

## AR / Local ML / Local LLM Fit

None load-bearing; the hard technical capability is a proper Elo/merge-sort-style pairwise ranking engine, not ML.

## Design Direction

Cinematic dark-mode-first system — near-black backgrounds, marquee-red accent, bold condensed poster-style typography, poster artwork as the dominant visual element, film-reel-inspired transitions.

## App Store Positioning

Star ratings lie to you. FaveOff makes you pick, one match-up at a time, until your real top 10 falls out.

## Invalidation Criteria

If users abandon mid-ranking after only a handful of comparisons and never reach a shareable top-10, the comparison flow is too tedious and needs a faster convergence algorithm or smaller default list size.

## v1 Build Scope

Add titles (movies+TV), head-to-head ranking engine, ranked list view, Taste Card generation + ShareLink, FaveOff+ paywall with StoreKit.

## Core Workflows

- add a watched title
- head-to-head comparison rounds
- browse computed ranked list
- generate & share Taste Card (poster grid of top 10)
- filter rankings by genre/decade

## iOS-Native Features

- ShareLink for Taste Card
- ImageRenderer for poster-grid card generation
- WidgetKit small widget showing current #1

## Key Risks

- The one network touchpoint (title/poster lookup) must degrade gracefully offline (initials/color-block placeholder), never blocking the core ranking loop
- Comparison fatigue on large libraries — needs a smart pairing algorithm scaling ~n log n, not full round-robin n²

## Claude Design Handoff Prompt

Design a cinematic entertainment-ranking app called FaveOff: near-black dark-mode-first backgrounds, marquee-red accent, bold condensed poster-style typography, movie/show poster art as the dominant visual content, film-reel-inspired swipe/transition motion for head-to-head comparison screens, feels like a red-carpet awards show, not a spreadsheet.

## Parent Portfolio Prompt

Build 5 completely separate, production-ready iOS apps at the same time.

This is a multi-app PORTFOLIO request: multiple apps in one program. Each selected app becomes its own separate project — one folder per app, never bundled together.

Pick any 5 categories/concepts — no fixed assignment. Choose the 5 strongest MASS-MARKET ideas.

TOP PRIORITY — VIRAL, MASS-MARKET APPEAL (hard requirement):
- Every app must target a BROAD, mainstream audience. NO niche-community apps (no hobbyist tools, no professional verticals, no special-interest utilities). If your parents, your barista, and a college student wouldn't all instantly get it, it fails.
- Each app must have a built-in viral loop: something people naturally share, screenshot, send to friends, or compete over (shareable results/cards, streaks, challenges, comparisons, "send this to a friend" moments).
- The concept must be explainable in one sentence and demo-able in 10 seconds.
- Think the scale of habit trackers, photo tools, social utilities, everyday life tools, casual self-improvement, money, food, sleep, relationships, entertainment — things with tens of millions of potential users.

SECOND PRIORITY — DESIGN & UI/UX:
- World-class, distinctive visual identity per app: deliberate color system, type scale, spacing, iconography, motion language — not stock SwiftUI defaults.
- "Apple Design Award" caliber polish. Every screen considered: empty, loading, error states, transitions, haptics, spring animations.

THIRD PRIORITY — COMPLEXITY & DEPTH:

- Depth that rewards daily use — but never at the expense of instant mass-market clarity.

FOURTH PRIORITY — MONETIZATION:
- Clear, sustainable revenue path per app (subscription tiers, IAP, premium) that feels native to the product, never punitive. Free tier hooks users; paid tier is compelling.

General Requirements:
1. Each app goes from 0 to production-ready.
2. Each app must be unique, useful, and commercially viable, better than competitors in a meaningful way.
3. Local-first, architected so cloud support can be added later without rewriting.

UNIQUENESS (hard rule, non-negotiable):
- No app in this batch may be similar to another app in this batch.
- No app may be similar to anything already built in this workspace: Waylay, TrueScale, Provenance, Scope-Guard, Practice-Loop, Proof, ReturnWise, VerveCoach, CueKeeper, brinekeeper, chalkline, quietpilot, cistern, thicket, undersong, undertow, heddle, marginalia, skyglass, and the ~50 apps of prior batches (aarti-path, afterglow, backtimer, chapterly, cleanline, clearpath-access, crockwatch, crosswire, crux-cycle, curio, dayframe, dharma-trail, docket, flinch, heartline, kitbag, night-sky-logbook, pattern-pulse, pillcheck, referral-loop, risk-compass, rollcall, runway, scenario-ledger, shelf, sincerely, tabzero, talkthrough, tessella, throughline, trestle, triptally, umbra, wordsmith-atlas, and others).
- Produce differentiated concepts even if a category overlaps.

Bonus points (optional, only when integral): unique use of LLMs, AR, or ML.

Build rules:
- Run the five app efforts in parallel; keep phases separate per app.
- TIME MATTERS on this batch: reach consensus quickly in discussion phases (end phases early on agreement), but never skip the build, and never mark an app done unless a real, compiling Xcode project with full source exists in app_build. An app without a buildable Xcode project is a FAILURE.
- Save all phase discussions; combine each app's transcript into a .txt at the end.
- Recover cleanly from stalls; report only important milestones.

Output:
- One folder per app in the output directory — never one folder containing all apps.
Output directory:
- /Users/pchordia/Documents/iOS-App-Factory

Now generate at least 10 candidate ideas, judge them primarily on mass-market viral potential, choose the best 5, and build all 5 to production quality with exceptional UI/UX.
```

## Phase Purpose

Create a production-grade product brief for this specific app: target user, paid value, core loop, subscription value, competitive wedge, viral or niche growth mechanism, local-first behavior, cloud-ready extension path, and the real reason the app deserves to exist.

## Transcript


### Round 1


**Codex (Design Lead · Systems Thinker) — Round 1**

FaveOff serves casual viewers who regularly watch movies and TV but never built a reliable personal “best of” system: people who finish a binge and can’t honestly say their own top 10 because 4-star ratings flatten nuance. The core use case is a weekly “I watched X last night” moment where adding a few new titles should immediately improve a living ranking and produce something they want to show in a chat, not a clerical review list.

Users pay for FaveOff+ when they hit two friction points in the free flow: they want more than movie-only ranking, and they want utility around depth. The paid unlocks a second ranking track for TV (free users can still log TV titles, but those aren’t included in ranked output), removes list-length caps for ranked tracks, enables genre/decade sub-rank views so “best rom-coms of 2010s” is fast, and unlocks periodic recap cards that feel like a monthly Wrapped moment. This keeps paid utility real and separate from core free value, instead of gating the initial experience.

The app’s core loop is intentionally tiny: add a watched title, resolve a fast head-to-head placement sequence, immediately insert it into a ranked list, then browse and share. The ranking engine is incremental binary insertion (bounded, on-device comparisons) so each new title is placed through a small, predictable number of matchups and never forced through a full round-robin treadmill. The comparison is the product: two posters, two large touch targets, one clean decision, then a clear placement confirmation (“#4 of 12, above ___ below ___”) before returning to browsing. If users cannot compare, a skip path exists that does not break ordering guarantees.

The subscription value is not cosmetic badges or social features; it is extra ranking surface and depth: more tracks, fewer constraints, richer recaps. Free users still get a complete, shareable movie taste loop with Taste Card export and offline-safe progress. If monetization doesn’t add actual decision power, it isn’t real value.

Competitive wedge is simple: this is not IMDb/Letterboxd/Rotten Tomatoes style cataloging and not social proof tracking. Those tools assume people already enjoy logging and rating. FaveOff is the opposite: it asks one tiny question repeatedly and turns that into a trustworthy taste model with native iOS UX and zero social graph. The wedge compounds through velocity and shareability: you don’t need followers to compete, just a well-made card to drop in text threads.

For growth, the strongest loop is share-first, screenshot-ready taste artifacts plus minimal friction referrals: “tap and compare to update your top 10, share your card, invite a friend to build theirs.” The viral moment is not a leaderboard; it’s social comparison by artifact. This keeps it mass-market, because everyone with a phone and a few watched titles can participate instantly.

The experience must feel local-first from first tap: all titles, comparisons, and ranking state are stored in SwiftData and remain usable offline. The network path for poster and metadata retrieval lives behind a protocol, with a local-first stub as primary behavior: manual title entry, clean placeholder posters from title initials and color blocks, and no blocking waits in ranking. Missing metadata should degrade to “compare anyway, enrich later,” never “cannot proceed.” Cloud is a clean extension path only: keep protocol boundaries around persistence and sync services so a future CloudKit layer can mirror local models without changing ranking logic.

The design should be cinematic by function, not just look: dark, high-contrast surfaces; a constrained high-impact red accent; poster-first cards; one-screen compare focus; short transitions that feel like a film reel cut; and immediate progression cues. The critical detail to preserve in later phases is a hard rule: every reachable screen has empty/loading/success/error states and 44-pt controls, with accessibility-first defaults. Biggest risk to this direction is trust collapse from sparse comparisons feeling arbitrary on edge-case pairings; if users stop after a few rounds, the ranking appears random and they quit. To prevent that, preserve the placed-feedback moment, strict comparison bounds, and a skip path that keeps ordering stable.

**Claude (QA & Risk · Closer) — Round 1**

Locking down the FaveOff product brief so this phase closes fast and clean, since everything upstream has already converged and there's nothing left to relitigate structurally — only to make concrete enough to build from.

The target user is a specific person, not a persona slide: someone who watches two or three things a week, has a strong reaction the moment the credits roll, and has never once been able to answer "okay but what's actually your favorite movie" with confidence. They're not a Letterboxd person, they don't want to log or review anything, and they will bounce immediately from any screen that smells like inventory management. Their use case is narrow: place the thing I just watched relative to the things I already know I liked, and eventually have something to show for it. That's the whole app. If I had to write the App Store subtitle myself it'd be "you already know which one was better."

Paid value has to be real utility, not a vanity unlock, or it fails the phase's own acceptance check. Free gets you: unlimited movie adds, full movie ranking via the comparison engine, browsing/filtering the movie list by genre and decade, and full Taste Card generation + sharing for the movies track. That's a complete, satisfying product on its own — I will not accept a free tier that's a teaser, because a crippled free tier is exactly what kills the viral loop this app depends on (a card someone's ashamed of because it's capped at 5 items doesn't get shared). FaveOff+ at $3.99/mo or $24.99/yr unlocks: the TV ranking track (TV titles can already be logged for free per our track-level gate decision, but ranking and TV Taste Cards are paid), unlimited list length if we do end up capping free movie lists (I'm deciding now that free is capped at 25 ranked movies — enough for a real top 10 to emerge multiple times over, not enough to feel unlimited), genre/decade sub-rankings as saved, nameable filtered views instead of one-off filters, and a periodic recap card ("Your Q3 Rewatch" style, reusing the same Taste Card renderer with a different data slice). Every one of those is functional depth, not a color swap or an ad removal — that's the bar and I'm holding it.

Core loop, stated as the thing that has to survive contact with a real screen: watch something, open the app, add it in under 10 seconds (title + type, offline placeholder art by default), get shown one comparison against a title already in your ranked list, tap the better one or hit skip, see the "placed" moment (rank number, what it beat, what beat it), and either keep comparing if the binary-insertion isn't done yet or land back on the list. That's it — no session structure beyond "you're done when the algorithm says you're done," bounded by log2(n) comparisons per insert, never more. My decision, closing an open question from earlier phases: default free list cap is 25 movies, not the unspecified "capped list length" the spec left vague — 25 is large enough to feel generous and small enough that a full re-rank sanity check stays fast and the Taste Card always has real depth to draw from.

Competitive wedge against the obvious comps: Letterboxd/IMDb ask you to already know your opinion and type it down as a number or a review; FaveOff produces the opinion by making you choose, which is a fundamentally different cognitive task — recognition over recall, exactly the thing that makes a personality quiz easier to finish than a form. Against Beli specifically (the closest real analog), the wedge is that Beli's category — restaurants — has no equivalent to a "Taste Card" as a static shareable object with the emotional weight of "these are the movies that define what I like"; posters carry identity in a way restaurant photos don't. Against generic bracket/tournament apps, the wedge is persistence — this isn't a one-time bracket that resets, it's a living ranked list that grows with you, which is the entire reason someone opens the app a second and fiftieth time.

Growth mechanism is viral, not niche, and it has to ride on the Taste Card specifically: the card is the whole product's proof-of-concept made visible, and the natural trigger is an argument — "no way you have [movie] above [movie]" is the exact reaction that makes someone screenshot-share it into a group chat, and the reply to that argument is "make your own list and prove me wrong," which is a built-in acquisition loop with zero social graph, zero friend-finding, zero account creation friction. I want to name the actual risk to this mechanism now rather than let it surface during build: if the Taste Card is generic-looking or doesn't clearly say "made in FaveOff," the share doesn't convert to installs — so the card needs a small persistent brand mark and needs to look good enough at native iMessage/Instagram-story thumbnail size, not just full-screen. That's a concrete visual requirement for the design phase, not a nice-to-have.

Local-first behavior: SwiftData is the only source of truth for titles, comparisons, and computed rank position — there is no server round-trip anywhere in the core loop, full stop. The cloud-ready path is a repository-boundary bet, not a built feature: the SwiftData models and ranking engine never reference CloudKit or any sync framework directly, so a future sync layer or account system slots in behind the existing repository protocol without touching ranking logic — I'm treating "could add CloudKit later without a rewrite" as an architectural constraint we verify by code review (is anything reaching into SwiftData directly from a view, or does everything go through a repository type), not as a feature we build now.

What makes someone keep using this past the novelty session, which is the thing I actually care about as the risk-focused voice in the room: it's not the paywall and it's not the widget, it's whether the "placed" moment feels genuinely rewarding every single time, because that's the only repeatable reward the app has — there's no streak, no notification nagging, no social feed to pull you back. If that moment is flat (just re-showing the list with the new item highlighted), retention dies quietly with no single obvious bug to point at. My closing decision for this phase: the placed-moment screen is a first-class, non-negotiable deliverable with its own designed state, not a derived view of the list — call it out explicitly in the brief so no later phase treats it as "just scroll to the new position." And the failure mode I want on record now, in QA terms, so it's testable later: if a user adds a title and it gets auto-placed without ever showing a comparison (because the list is empty or has only one item), the placed-moment copy must still say something coherent ("first one in — add another to start ranking") rather than a broken "#1 of 1, ahead of nothing, behind nothing" string, because that null-state sentence is exactly the kind of thing that ships broken if nobody names it as a case in the brief.

**Coordinator (Claude) — decision after round 1**

Both speakers landed in the same place again — this is the fourth phase in a row where Codex lays out the framework and Claude locks specific numbers/decisions on top of it without contradicting anything. No open fight here.

CONSENSUS: YES

## Final Output

**Target user & use case:** Someone who watches 2-3 things a week, has a strong reaction the moment the credits roll, but has never been able to confidently answer "what's actually your favorite movie." Not a Letterboxd person, not a completionist — they'll bounce off anything that smells like inventory management or logging. The use case is narrow and recurring: place the thing I just watched relative to what I already know I liked, and eventually have something worth showing off. Working App Store subtitle: "You already know which one was better."

**Paid value & subscription value:** Free tier is a genuinely complete, satisfying product — unlimited movie adds, full movie ranking via the comparison engine, genre/decade filtering, and full Taste Card generation + sharing for the movies track, capped at 25 ranked movies (large enough for a real top 10 to surface repeatedly, small enough to keep re-rank fast and leave paid list-length removal meaningful). A crippled free tier would kill the viral loop this app depends on, so the cap is generous by design, not a teaser. FaveOff+ ($3.99/mo or $24.99/yr) unlocks real functional depth, not cosmetics: the TV ranking track (TV titles can already be logged for free per the track-level gate; ranking and TV Taste Cards are paid), removal of the 25-item cap, saved/nameable genre-decade sub-ranking views instead of one-off filters, and periodic recap cards ("Your Q3 Rewatch") reusing the same Taste Card renderer against a different data slice.

**Core loop:** Watch something → add it in under 10 seconds (title + type, offline placeholder art by default) → one comparison at a time against the existing ranked list via bounded binary-insertion (skip/"too close to call" escape available) → the **Placed** moment as a first-class, purpose-built screen state — not a derived view of the list — showing rank number, what it beat, what beat it → back to browsing, or continue if insertion isn't done. Bounded at log2(n) comparisons per insert, never more. Cold-start/edge case locked now: if a title is auto-placed without a comparison (empty list or single-item list), the Placed copy must be coherent ("first one in — add another to start ranking"), not a broken "#1 of 1, ahead of nothing" string.

**Competitive wedge:** Letterboxd/IMDb/Rotten Tomatoes assume you already know your opinion and ask you to type it as a number or review — recall. FaveOff produces the opinion by making you choose — recognition, the same reason personality quizzes finish and forms don't. Against Beli (the closest real analog), the wedge is that posters carry identity weight restaurant photos never will, so the Taste Card has more emotional pull as a shareable object. Against bracket/tournament apps, the wedge is persistence — this is a living list that grows with you, not a one-shot bracket that resets, which is why someone opens it a second and fiftieth time.

**Viral/growth angle:** The Taste Card is the growth loop, not a leaderboard or social graph. The trigger is disagreement — "no way you have [movie] above [movie]" — and the natural reply is "make your own and prove me wrong," a zero-friction acquisition loop with no account creation or friend-finding required. Concrete design requirement flowing out of this: the card needs a small persistent brand mark and must read clearly at iMessage/Instagram-story thumbnail size, not just full-screen — if it looks generic or unbranded, the share doesn't convert to installs.

**Local-first & cloud-ready plan:** SwiftData is the sole source of truth for titles, comparisons, and computed rank position — no server round-trip anywhere in the core loop. Poster/metadata network lookup sits behind a protocol with an offline-first stub (manual entry, initials/color-block art) as the default happy path; missing metadata means "compare anyway, enrich later," never "cannot proceed." Cloud-readiness is an architectural bet enforced by code review, not a built feature: no view or ranking code may reach into SwiftData directly — everything routes through a repository protocol, so a future CloudKit sync layer or account system can slot in later without touching ranking logic.

**Quality Gate (Claude) — after round 1**

QUALITY: PASS

## Feedback
This output meets the phase bar. All six required elements are covered with concrete, app-specific detail rather than generic category description: a precisely drawn target user with a working subtitle, a paid/subscription section that names functional unlocks (TV ranking track, cap removal, saved sub-rank views, recap cards) rather than cosmetics, a core loop stated in buildable terms (add → bounded binary-insertion compare → first-class Placed state → browse → share) with the cold-start edge case explicitly resolved, a competitive wedge argued against three distinct comp categories (Letterboxd/IMDb, Beli, bracket apps) rather than asserted, a viral growth angle with a concrete design requirement flowing from it (thumbnail-legible brand mark on the card), and a local-first/cloud-ready plan that specifies the architectural enforcement mechanism (repository boundary, verified by code review) rather than just asserting "it's local-first."

It correctly carries forward every locked decision from prior phases (track-level TV gate, bounded merge-insertion with skip escape, offline-first metadata stub, non-blocking sandbox paywall) without contradiction, and resolves the one previously-open numeric gap (spec's vague "capped list length") with a specific, defended number (25 movies) instead of leaving it for a later phase to guess. The brief is concrete enough to hand to a design phase without further ideation. No repair needed.

## Final Output

**Target user & use case:** Someone who watches 2-3 things a week, has a strong reaction the moment the credits roll, but has never been able to confidently answer "what's actually your favorite movie." Not a Letterboxd person, not a completionist — they'll bounce off anything that smells like inventory management or logging. The use case is narrow and recurring: place the thing I just watched relative to what I already know I liked, and eventually have something worth showing off. Working App Store subtitle: "You already know which one was better."

**Paid value & subscription value:** Free tier is a genuinely complete, satisfying product — unlimited movie adds, full movie ranking via the comparison engine, genre/decade filtering, and full Taste Card generation + sharing for the movies track, capped at 25 ranked movies (large enough for a real top 10 to surface repeatedly, small enough to keep re-rank fast and leave paid list-length removal meaningful). A crippled free tier would kill the viral loop this app depends on, so the cap is generous by design, not a teaser. FaveOff+ ($3.99/mo or $24.99/yr) unlocks real functional depth, not cosmetics: the TV ranking track (TV titles can already be logged for free per the track-level gate; ranking and TV Taste Cards are paid), removal of the 25-item cap, saved/nameable genre-decade sub-ranking views instead of one-off filters, and periodic recap cards ("Your Q3 Rewatch") reusing the same Taste Card renderer against a different data slice.

**Core loop:** Watch something → add it in under 10 seconds (title + type, offline placeholder art by default) → one comparison at a time against the existing ranked list via bounded binary-insertion (skip/"too close to call" escape available) → the **Placed** moment as a first-class, purpose-built screen state — not a derived view of the list — showing rank number, what it beat, what beat it → back to browsing, or continue if insertion isn't done. Bounded at log2(n) comparisons per insert, never more. Cold-start/edge case locked now: if a title is auto-placed without a comparison (empty list or single-item list), the Placed copy must be coherent ("first one in — add another to start ranking"), not a broken "#1 of 1, ahead of nothing" string.

**Competitive wedge:** Letterboxd/IMDb/Rotten Tomatoes assume you already know your opinion and ask you to type it as a number or review — recall. FaveOff produces the opinion by making you choose — recognition, the same reason personality quizzes finish and forms don't. Against Beli (the closest real analog), the wedge is that posters carry identity weight restaurant photos never will, so the Taste Card has more emotional pull as a shareable object. Against bracket/tournament apps, the wedge is persistence — this is a living list that grows with you, not a one-shot bracket that resets, which is why someone opens it a second and fiftieth time.

**Viral/growth angle:** The Taste Card is the growth loop, not a leaderboard or social graph. The trigger is disagreement — "no way you have [movie] above [movie]" — and the natural reply is "make your own and prove me wrong," a zero-friction acquisition loop with no account creation or friend-finding required. Concrete design requirement flowing out of this: the card needs a small persistent brand mark and must read clearly at iMessage/Instagram-story thumbnail size, not just full-screen — if it looks generic or unbranded, the share doesn't convert to installs.

**Local-first & cloud-ready plan:** SwiftData is the sole source of truth for titles, comparisons, and computed rank position — no server round-trip anywhere in the core loop. Poster/metadata network lookup sits behind a protocol with an offline-first stub (manual entry, initials/color-block art) as the default happy path; missing metadata means "compare anyway, enrich later," never "cannot proceed." Cloud-readiness is an architectural bet enforced by code review, not a built feature: no view or ranking code may reach into SwiftData directly — everything routes through a repository protocol, so a future CloudKit sync layer or account system can slot in later without touching ranking logic.

---

CONSENSUS: YES

# faveoff — Next Steps Small

_Generated by the autonomous multi-agent orchestrator on 2026-07-08 12:21:47._

## Original Prompt

```
PORTFOLIO_CHILD_PROJECT: true
Parent portfolio: multi-app-exp7
Selected app slug: faveoff

Build this app as a working local-first SwiftUI MVP.

## Selected App Spec

# FaveOff

Build mode: **MVP build**.
Build priority: **4**.

## App Name

FaveOff

## Category

Entertainment / Taste Ranking

## One-Sentence Promise

Rank the movies and shows you've watched head-to-head, and FaveOff builds your real, personal top 10 — then turns it into a shareable taste card.

## Target User

Anyone who watches movies/shows and has opinions but finds star ratings meaningless and social-network movie apps like overhead — mass-market watchers, not cinephile completionists.

## Painful Moment Solved

Rating a movie 1-5 stars and realizing that number means nothing next to the 40 other movies you've also rated 4 stars — you genuinely don't know your own top 10.

## Why It Is Different

Instead of star ratings, shows two watched titles and asks which was better, building an Elo-style ranked list from a handful of quick head-to-head picks — far more decisive than star ratings, ending in a poster-grid 'Taste Card' built for sharing, with zero social graph or account required to get value.

## Why Users Would Pay

Free tier ranks movies only with a capped list length; FaveOff+ unlocks TV shows and music/albums as separate ranking tracks, unlimited list length, genre-filtered sub-rankings, and periodic 'Wrapped'-style recap cards.

## Subscription Model

Freemium, $3.99/mo or $24.99/yr.

## Local-First MVP Scope

SwiftData store for titles, comparisons, and computed rankings; ranking algorithm runs fully on-device; title/poster metadata lookup is the one network dependency, isolated behind a protocol.

## Future Backend Roadmap

Repository boundary allows CloudKit sync across devices and, later, an optional social taste-comparison layer without touching the core ranking engine.

## AR / Local ML / Local LLM Fit

None load-bearing; the hard technical capability is a proper Elo/merge-sort-style pairwise ranking engine, not ML.

## Design Direction

Cinematic dark-mode-first system — near-black backgrounds, marquee-red accent, bold condensed poster-style typography, poster artwork as the dominant visual element, film-reel-inspired transitions.

## App Store Positioning

Star ratings lie to you. FaveOff makes you pick, one match-up at a time, until your real top 10 falls out.

## Invalidation Criteria

If users abandon mid-ranking after only a handful of comparisons and never reach a shareable top-10, the comparison flow is too tedious and needs a faster convergence algorithm or smaller default list size.

## v1 Build Scope

Add titles (movies+TV), head-to-head ranking engine, ranked list view, Taste Card generation + ShareLink, FaveOff+ paywall with StoreKit.

## Core Workflows

- add a watched title
- head-to-head comparison rounds
- browse computed ranked list
- generate & share Taste Card (poster grid of top 10)
- filter rankings by genre/decade

## iOS-Native Features

- ShareLink for Taste Card
- ImageRenderer for poster-grid card generation
- WidgetKit small widget showing current #1

## Key Risks

- The one network touchpoint (title/poster lookup) must degrade gracefully offline (initials/color-block placeholder), never blocking the core ranking loop
- Comparison fatigue on large libraries — needs a smart pairing algorithm scaling ~n log n, not full round-robin n²

## Claude Design Handoff Prompt

Design a cinematic entertainment-ranking app called FaveOff: near-black dark-mode-first backgrounds, marquee-red accent, bold condensed poster-style typography, movie/show poster art as the dominant visual content, film-reel-inspired swipe/transition motion for head-to-head comparison screens, feels like a red-carpet awards show, not a spreadsheet.

General Requirements:
```
