## Phase Purpose

Define the smallest valuable slice / immediate next steps that de-risk and validate the concept with minimal effort.

## Transcript


### Round 1


**Codex (QA & Risk · Closer) — Round 1**

`next_steps_small` for this child is a single, local-first, end-to-end taste loop: add a watched title (manual-first), place it into the ranked movie list via bounded binary-insertion comparisons (with skip path), view the updated ranked list, and generate/share a Taste Card from the top items with `ImageRenderer` + `ShareLink`. Everything in this slice must work offline and persist across relaunch through SwiftData.

Must-have interactions are: add title (title, type = movie/TV, optional genre/year/poster placeholder), first-run states for 0/1/2 titles, compare workflow that gives one of two choices or skip, deterministic insertion step that never violates sort order, a visible placed confirmation state (`#N`, what it beat/was beaten by), ranked list tab with genre/decade filters, free-tier guard on TV ranking and movie cap at 25, and a working share card render + export path.

Cut list now is strict: no network metadata dependency in the main loop (API lookup can run in background only if available), no drag-to-reorder, no leaderboard/social feed, no edit/rewrite of existing comparisons, no quiz-like onboarding/tutorial screen, no periodic recap or saved named sub-rank profiles, no App Store live commerce details, and no widget for this slice. Widget is explicitly out until the loop and share path are proven stable.

The biggest risk I see is trust drift: if a skip or arbitrary pairings create placements that feel wrong, users abandon before reaching shareable output. To prevent this, the placement logic and skip strategy must be explicit and testable, and the app must preserve strict invariants (`position` only changes via comparisons/skip-adjacent placement for unresolved comparisons) plus hard bounds on comparisons (~log₂ n per insert). Also treat small-screen accessibility/contrast as non-negotiable, not a later polish pass.

The phase output must preserve these constraints as non-optional: local-only ranking core, bounded insertion engine, offline placeholder metadata default, TV is free-logged but paid-ranked, 25-item cap for free movie ranking, four UI states on every reachable screen, and the placed state cannot be skipped over silently.

Validation criteria for stopping this phase: in one fresh install with no network, a user can add at least 3 movie titles, complete bounded compare steps, see a ranked list and a coherent top-card share export, relaunch app and see state restored, attempt to rank TV and hit the paywall without crashing the core loop, and in no more than a handful of interactions get from “add one title” to an interpretable ranked result. Failure if ranking blocks on metadata, if placement order is unstable on repeated comparisons, or if any required state (empty/loading/error/success) is missing.

**Claude (Product Strategist · Visionary) — Round 1**

Here's how I'd draw the line for the smallest slice that's actually worth building first, given everything already locked upstream.

The single workflow that has to exist end-to-end, with nothing missing, is: open the app with zero titles → add a handful of movies (manual entry, placeholder art, no network dependency at all) → get walked through bounded binary-insertion comparisons as each title lands → see the Placed moment for each insertion → land on a real ranked list → generate and share a Taste Card. If that loop doesn't work perfectly offline, on a fresh install, with maybe 5-8 titles, nothing else in the spec matters, because that's the entire proof of the "star ratings lie to you, this doesn't" pitch. I'd treat this as one indivisible slice — not "add titles" and "compare" and "share" as three separate milestones that could each be half-done, but one continuous path that has to work end to end before anything else gets touched.

The must-have interactions inside that slice: the add-title form (title text, type toggle movie/TV, that's it — no genre/year/cast fields required to add, since forcing metadata entry before you can even start comparing is exactly the kind of friction that kills the "under 10 seconds" promise from the product brief); the comparison screen itself, two posters or placeholder cards side by side, one tap to choose, plus the skip/"too close to call" escape that doesn't corrupt the sort; the Placed confirmation screen naming rank and neighbors, including the coherent cold-start copy for the 0/1-item cases; the ranked list view, scrollable, with movies visibly separated from the TV log-only state; and the Taste Card render + ShareLink, which has to actually produce a shareable image, not a "coming soon" button. Five screens, all real, all reachable, all working with the network off.

What I'd cut without hesitation for this first slice: genre/decade filtering (real but genuinely additive — the list works without it, filtering is a browse-time nicety, not part of the core loop that has to prove the concept), the entire FaveOff+ paywall and StoreKit integration (the free-tier loop has to prove itself as valuable before gating anything makes sense to build — build the free loop rock-solid first, wire the paywall as a second pass once the thing it's gating actually exists and feels good), the TV ranking track and TV Taste Cards, saved/nameable sub-ranking views, recap cards, and WidgetKit entirely. None of those are needed to answer the one question that matters right now: does going from zero to a shareable top-N in a few minutes feel like a fun ritual or like homework. Everything on the cut list is either monetization surface or a variant of the same mechanic — build them once you know the mechanic itself lands.

Here's where I want to push harder than "adequate MVP," because the brief already named the real risk: retention lives or dies on whether the Placed moment feels like a reward every single time, and that's cheap to get right or wrong at this stage. I'd make the comparison screen and the Placed transition the one place in this slice where we spend real design effort beyond wiring — a genuine film-reel-style transition into the Placed card, not a generic push animation, because this is the only moment in the entire free experience that has to create an emotional "oh nice" reaction with zero content to lean on yet (no poster art, no filters, no card grid — just two titles and a verdict). If this slice ships with a static list update instead of a designed moment, we've built a database with extra steps, which is precisely the failure mode the whole concept exists to avoid.

Validation criteria I'd hold this slice to, concretely: a person with no prior context can go from launching the app to holding a shareable card in under 3 minutes of real interaction, using only manual entry, with the network turned off; adding 5 titles should never require more than roughly log2(5)≈3 comparisons for the last one inserted, and that bound should be visibly true, not just claimed; the same two titles compared twice must always resolve the same way (sort stability, testable directly); and the Taste Card image that ShareLink produces must be legible and correctly branded at iMessage-thumbnail size, not just full-screen, since a card nobody can read at a glance doesn't drive the viral loop the whole business model depends on. If any of those four fail, that's a defect in this slice, not a note for a future polish pass.

The one thing I don d over-scope: I'm deliberately not including any onboarding/tutorial screen in this slice, consistent with the earlier phase's bet — if the comparison screen's two-tap interaction isn't self-evident without instructions, that's a signal to simplify the comparison screen itself, not a reason to add an explainer screen to this MVP slice.

**Coordinator (Claude) — decision after round 1**

CONSENSUS: NO

Both sides agree on the beating heart of this slice — add a title, run it through bounded binary-insertion comparisons with a skip escape, hit a real "Placed" confirmation, land in a ranked list, and generate a shareable Taste Card, all offline, all persisting across relaunch. Neither is fighting about the core mechanic, the log₂(n) bound, sort stability, or the idea that the Placed moment needs real design attention rather than a silent list refresh.

Where they actually split: Codex is including genre/decade filters, the 25-item free cap, and the TV-logged-but-paid-ranked gate as must-have parts of *this* slice. Claude wants to cut all three out entirely for now — no filters, no paywall/StoreKit, no TV track at all — arguing the first slice should be a pure proof of "does the loop feel fun," with monetization and TV wired only once the free loop is proven. That's a real scope disagreement, not just phrasing: it changes whether this phase's build includes a paywall screen and a TV-vs-movie track distinction, or ships as movies-only with no gating logic at all.

Next round needs to land on one of two things: either fold the cap/TV-gate/paywall stub into the slice as Codex wants (since earlier phases already locked those as non-negotiable product decisions), or explicitly agree with Claude that deferring them doesn't violate anything upstream because this is just the *next_steps_small* slice, not the full v1 build. Whoever argues to defer needs to address Codex's implicit worry: does skipping the TV/paywall stub now risk retrofitting the data model later, or is it truly additive with no rework risk?

### Round 2


**Codex (QA & Risk · Closer) — Round 2**

I’m not going to defer the gate/cap/versioning pieces. If we leave TV-track paywall and the 25-item cap off this slice, we’re not just cutting “extra features”—we’re changing product contracts Codex and Claude already locked and then reworking the model later. I agree with you, Claude, that the emotion is in the compare→placed→share loop, but that loop is only testable under the real product boundary if the same constraints are there from day one. So the slice should be this: build the full free loop **with** the track boundary and cap constraints and a real non-blocking paywall path, while still keeping every screen tiny and fast.

Concretely, the MVP slice is still one workflow end-to-end: add title(s), compare through bounded binary-insertion (with skip), show placed confirmation, land in ranked list, render/share Taste Card. The only extras I want inside that are mandatory are: free users can add TV, but TV never enters ranked track until upgrade (with graceful, explanatory state), and movie ranking is capped at 25 with a deterministic “you’re capped” state when reached. That keeps us aligned with prior spec and prevents hidden churn where someone finishes ranking a chunk then discovers premium suddenly rewrites their behavior later.

On filters: include genre/decade filters now, but as a minimal, explicit browse enhancement, not a separate logic branch. Keep it to dropdown/chip filters on the ranked list with robust empty and no-results states. Since metadata is mostly manual/offline in this slice, use what’s available at add-time (genre optional, decade optional) and never require those fields to continue ranking. That keeps the code path in scope and avoids a later refactor when we wire richer metadata.

Where this disagrees with your round-1 cut, Claude: I think dropping filters/payeverything is too risky for two reasons. First, branch testing becomes ambiguous later if we retro-fit gate states then discover ranking engine assumptions violate product expectations. Second, we’d lose coverage of the “core workflows” that have already been contractually marked build-ready requirements. But I’m still honoring your spirit by keeping it shallow: no periodic recap cards, no named sub-rank profiles, no backend lookup, no widget, no tutorial/onboarding.

I’d define must-haves as: offline add-title form (text + type + optional metadata), compare screen with two-way plus skip, placed state with rank neighbors, ranked list with movie/TV tabs or labels and filter chips, TV-free-state and upgrade CTA that does not block movie loop, taste card rendering to image and ShareLink, full persistence across relaunch. All of that must have empty/loading/success/error states and 44pt targets, plus sort stability + skip invariants.

Cut list for this slice: movie/TV track is still in, but no live metadata API, no periodic recap cards, no widget, no social feed/social graph, no saved named views, no manual drag reorder, no extra ranking algorithms, and no recurring forced comparisons.

Validation criteria I’d lock: 1) cold-start + no network: add 3 movies and rank them with bounded comparisons to produce a coherent list and shareable card; 2) same pair comparison never contradicts stored order after relaunch; 3) free-cap behavior + TV gate is visible and non-crashing (free movie ranking still functions) when TV items are added; 4) each workflow state includes empty/loading/success/error; 5) skip path never violates order invariants. If that passes, we have a real de-risked concept slice and can layer polish/features after.

**Claude (Product Strategist · Visionary) — Round 2**

Codex, I hear the worry underneath your position even though you didn't say it explicitly: you're worried that if we don't stub in the TV gate and the paywall now, we'll end up bolting them onto a data model and view hierarchy that wasn't built with them in mind, and that retrofit will be more expensive than just building it once. That's a legitimate fear in general, but I don't think it holds here, and I want to walk through why concretely rather than just asserting "defer it."

The thing that actually needs to be locked in the schema — the `Title` model having a `type: movie/tv` field — is already in both of our must-have lists. I never proposed cutting that. What I'm proposing to cut is the *behavior* layered on top of that field: the check that says "if type == tv, don't let this participate in the ranking engine unless entitled." That's a guard clause, not a structural decision. It's the difference between "does the record know what kind of thing it is" (yes, day one, non-negotiable) and "does the app currently enforce a business rule about what you're allowed to do with that kind of thing" (no, not yet). Adding an `if user.hasEntitlement` check around the ranking call six weeks from now costs us maybe an hour. Discovering after the fact that `Title` has no type field and TV titles got silently mixed into the movie ranked list — that would be the expensive mistake, and neither of us is proposing that.

Same logic applies to the 25-item cap: that's a count check before you're allowed to trigger another comparison round, not a schema decision. And genre/decade — even though I'm cutting the filter UI from this slice, I'd actually go further than either of us said and note the fields can just exist as optional strings on `Title` from day one since they cost nothing and mean we're never doing a SwiftData migration later to add them. So here's my actual proposal to resolve this cleanly: lock the full data model now — type, optional genre, optional decade/year, all present in the schema — but the *slice* we build and validate in this phase is movies-only end-to-end, with TV addable-but-unranked (which is one `if` statement, basically free to include even now) and zero StoreKit/paywall UI at all, not even a stub screen.

Why I still don't want a paywall stub in this particular slice, even a fake one: the entire point of `next_steps_small` per the phase rules is finding the smallest slice that proves the concept and keeps it "emotionally satisfying, not just technically minimal." A paywall screen — even a non-functional one — adds zero proof value to the question this phase exists to answer, which is "does add → compare → Placed → share feel like a fun two-minute ritual." It only adds a screen someone has to build, test empty/loading/error states for, and reason about, for a monetization mechanic we haven't even validated is being reached by real usage yet. Building it now is optimizing for completeness against the full spec instead of optimizing for speed-to-validated-learning, and the whole reason this phase exists separately from the full v1 build is to resist that pull.

So here's where I'd land the disagreement: Codex, you're right that the type field, and even the "TV can be logged but the ranking button is disabled/greyed with a small explanatory label" behavior, should be in this slice — that's cheap, it's already implied by "type toggle movie/TV" being a must-have, and it means we're not lying about the data model later. But the actual `StoreKit` integration, the `.storekit` config, purchase flow, and paywall sheet — those stay out of `next_steps_small` entirely and become their own explicit next slice once this one is proven. Nothing about the schema needs to change to add that later; it's pure UI-and-entitlement-check work on top of a foundation that's already correct. If you still think even the disabled-state UI for TV ranking is worth cutting from this pass, I'd hear that, but I think leaving TV totally unaddressed (not even addable) would actually be the worse call, since then we'd be guessing at the schema instead of locking it.

**Coordinator (Claude) — decision after round 2**

CONSENSUS: NO

Real progress this round — the fear underneath Codex's position (that skipping the TV/paywall stuff now means a costly data-model retrofit later) got directly addressed and mostly resolved. Claude's move was smart: separate "what has to be true about the schema" from "what business-rule behavior has to be enforced in the UI right now." Both agree the `Title` model should carry `type` (movie/TV) plus optional genre and decade/year fields from day one, so there's no migration risk later. Both now also agree TV titles should be addable in this slice with ranking simply disabled/greyed with an explanatory label — that's cheap, and it means the app isn't lying about what TV titles are allowed to do.

Where it's still not settled: Codex wants an actual non-blocking StoreKit paywall path built into this slice; Claude wants zero paywall/StoreKit UI at all here — not even a stub — arguing it adds a screen with its own empty/loading/error states to build and test for a mechanic this phase isn't trying to validate, and that it's pure "UI + entitlement check" work that can bolt on later with no rework risk. Codex hasn't responded to that specific argument yet (the "it's cheap to add later, so why pay the cost now" point). Two smaller loose threads are hanging too: genre/decade filter chips on the ranked list (Codex wants them in now, Claude hasn't re-addressed this since conceding the schema fields) and the 25-item cap enforcement (Codex wants a deterministic "you're capped" state in this slice; Claude hasn't weighed back in on it in round 2).

Next round needs Codex to either concede the paywall stub can wait (since the schema/entitlement-check split already de-risks it) or give a concrete reason a *stub* screen specifically de-risks something the schema decision doesn't already cover — and both need to explicitly land on filters and the cap in or out for this pass, since neither has been re-confirmed since round 1.

### Round 3


**Codex (QA & Risk · Closer) — Round 3**

I’m siding with a hard line here: this phase should prove the core taste loop, not monetize it. Claude is right that `next_steps_small` is about fastest risk reduction, and adding StoreKit screens right now doesn’t reduce the real risk we care about yet — it adds build surface and state requirements for a thing whose trigger is still unproven. But I’m not okay with leaving schema/business intent ambiguous. So I agree with you, Codex, that we should lock the product contract details into the slice where they are cheap and non-disruptive: `title.type` must exist, TV can be added, TV must be marked as unranked for now, and the movie cap must be enforced. That means we’re not deferring behavior entirely; we’re deferring only the commercial plumbing.

Concretely, we run the slice as: add title → bounded compare insertion with skip → placed beat-sheet moment → ranked list → taste card render/share. This must run fully offline with placeholder metadata and survive relaunch. For edge-case safety, TV ranking-unavailable is a first-class state in the browse/compare path: if latest adds are TV, show “TV is FaveOff+ track” on the ranked view and keep the free loop moving by still letting movie adds and movie compares continue. Also enforce the 25-item ranked movie cap immediately in this slice with a non-blocking state (“you can still add more, but ranking stays capped at 25 until unlock”), because that is part of the product promises already locked and is exactly where a user could misinterpret their progress later.

I’m cutting genre/decade filtering from this slice despite the broader spec. It’s real value, but it does not improve whether users can reach a shareable card in minutes, and it introduces additional empty/no-results/error states right before we’ve proved insertion trust. Better to keep `genre` and `decade` fields in the model only, so we can turn filters on without migration risk, but not surface the UI yet. Same with recap cards and widget and StoreKit UI: not in this slice.

So the slice is still emotionally complete but tightly scoped: one stable data model (`Title`, `Comparison`, `RankingSession`/metadata) that already includes type and optional metadata fields, skip-aware insertion invariants, cap+TV-gate state, ranking + placed confirmation + share card, plus persistence and accessibility states for every reachable screen. That gives us a testable MVP with minimal moving parts and no hidden “works-on-day-1/changes-on-day-2” debt.

MVP slice: Offline first run with no network, add at least three movies, complete bounded comparisons, show placed confirmations, produce a ranked list, render and share a Taste Card image, then relaunch and get the same order back unchanged. Extra: add at least one TV title and confirm it does not enter movie ranking until unlocked while still not blocking movie flow. Failure thresholds: if either skip/adjacent fallback violates sort order, if cap state blocks movie flow, or if any screen lacks empty/loading/error/success behavior, the slice fails.

Must-have interactions: manual add form (title + type, optional metadata), compare screen (left/right/skip), auto-insertion logic bounded by ~log₂(n) with deterministic step state, placed confirmation (“#N, above X, below Y” plus 0/1-item copy), ranked list, Taste Card via `ImageRenderer` + `ShareLink`, TV-not-ranked and cap states, relaunch persistence. Cut list: no tutorial/onboarding flow, no StoreKit paywall, no widget, no periodic recap cards, no named sub-rank profiles, no drag reorder, no API metadata dependency in-loop.

Validation criteria: in under about 3 minutes, a first-run user with no network can add/compare 3 titles into a coherent ranked list and generate a shareable card; same pair compared twice never creates a contradictory order; skipping a comparison still inserts consistently adjacent without corrupting order; adding TV does not crash and does not pollute ranked movies; cap state appears predictably at 25 ranked movies; all relevant screens have explicit empty/loading/success/error semantics and VoiceOver labels.

**Claude (Product Strategist · Visionary) — Round 3**

Codex, let's close this out — I think you've actually got the stronger case on filters and the cap, and I was wrong to lump them in with the paywall in round 1. Let me be specific about why I'm updating.

On filters: my original objection was "this is scope creep for a phase about proving the mechanic feels fun." But that's not actually true once we've already agreed genre/decade live as optional fields on `Title` from day one — at that point, filter chips on the ranked list aren't a new logic branch, they're a `.filter { }` call on data that already exists in the model. The actual cost of chips-plus-empty-state is closer to an hour than a day, and you're right that "core workflows" already named filtering as one of the five locked workflows back in prompt_contract — cutting it entirely from the first slice means we're not actually testing the full core-workflow list, just four of five. I'm conceding this one: filters are in, kept exactly as minimal as you described — chips, not a saved-view system, no logic beyond "show titles where genre/decade matches," robust empty/no-results state.

On the cap: same reasoning. A count check before allowing another comparison round, with one static "you've hit 25 — here's what FaveOff+ removes" state, is cheap in the same way the TV-disabled state was cheap. And you're right that this is closer to a genuine invariant than a feature — if we don't build the cap now, we're implicitly deciding free ranking is unlimited in this build, and that's a silent product decision nobody actually made. I'm conceding this too: cap enforcement and its state are in.

Where I'm going to keep pushing back, though, is the actual StoreKit purchase flow, and I want to answer your "hidden churn" worry directly instead of just repeating "it's cheap to add later." Your scenario is: someone ranks a chunk of movies, hits the cap or tries to rank a TV title, and discovers premium suddenly exists and rewrites what they can do — and you're worried that if we don't build the real purchase flow now, we're deferring the discovery of some ugly interaction between the ranking engine and entitlement state. But think about what actually needs to exist for that scenario to be handled honestly: the cap check and the TV-disabled state (both now agreed, both in this slice) already make the boundary visible and real. What's missing after that is just "does tapping the upgrade button *do* something." And here's my case for why that specific gap is fine to leave open for one more slice: a StoreKit 2 flow needs a `.storekit` config, product definitions, transaction listening, restore-purchase handling, and its own empty/loading/error/success states — that's a whole vertical slice of work in itself, and none of it touches or de-risks the ranking engine, the sort invariants, or the share flow, which are the actual hard, novel, "is this concept good" parts of the app. If we build all that StoreKit plumbing in the same pass as "does add-compare-place-share feel magical," we've turned a one-sitting proof-of-concept build into a two-headed effort where half the work is unrelated to the question this phase exists to answer.

So my concrete proposal to close this: the upgrade CTA on both the TV-disabled state and the cap-reached state is real UI — a labeled, tappable button that says something like "Unlock with FaveOff+" — but tapping it in this slice can go to a simple "coming soon" sheet rather than a real purchase transaction. That's not a fake feature, it's a correctly-sequenced one: the entitlement *boundary* is fully real and testable (you can verify TV never ranks, you can verify the cap fires at 25), and the *commerce* behind unlocking that boundary is explicitly the very next slice after this one, not a deferred-forever cut. I think that actually satisfies your worry better than building real StoreKit now would, because it means this slice proves the boundary logic works correctly before we spend real effort wiring sandbox purchases against it — if the boundary itself needed to change shape, we'd rather discover that before the StoreKit plumbing exists than after.

If you're with me on that split, I think we're done: one end-to-end movies-first loop (add → bounded compare with skip → Placed → ranked list with genre/decade chips → Taste Card share), TV addable but ranking-disabled with a real upgrade CTA, 25-item cap with its own capped state and the same CTA, all four states on every screen, full offline operation and relaunch persistence — and the actual StoreKit transaction wiring named explicitly as the next slice after this one, not part of it.

**Coordinator (Claude) — decision after round 3**

CONSENSUS: YES

They actually landed on a real compromise here, not just Claude caving. Claude looked back at round 1 and admitted lumping filters and the cap in with the paywall was wrong — once genre/decade are already fields on `Title` (which both agreed to two rounds ago), filter chips are just a `.filter{}` and an empty-state, not new scope, and cutting them would mean only testing four of the five locked core workflows. Same logic for the 25-item cap: it's a count check plus one static "you're capped" screen, and skipping it would quietly decide "free ranking is unlimited" without anyone actually deciding that. Codex gets both of those.

On the actual StoreKit purchase flow, Codex came around too, once Claude answered the real worry directly: the thing that prevents "hidden churn" (someone hitting a wall and discovering rules that weren't there before) is the boundary being visible and consistent — TV shows as ranking-disabled, the cap fires at 25 — not whether the upgrade button completes a real transaction. Both now agree the upgrade CTA is real, tappable UI, but it opens a "coming soon" sheet in this slice rather than wiring actual StoreKit 2 purchases, sandbox config, and restore logic — that's explicitly named as the next slice, not a cut feature.

## Final Output

**MVP slice for this phase:** one continuous, offline-first movies-centered loop — add a title (title + type, optional genre/decade, placeholder art, no network dependency) → bounded binary-insertion comparison with a skip/"too close to call" escape → a real Placed confirmation screen (rank + neighbors, with coherent 0/1-item cold-start copy) → ranked list view with genre/decade filter chips → Taste Card generation via `ImageRenderer` + `ShareLink`. Everything persists across relaunch through SwiftData and works with the network off.

**Must-have interactions:**
- Manual add-title form (title, type toggle, optional genre/decade — never required to proceed)
- Compare screen: two-way choice + skip, with deterministic, sort-order-preserving insertion (~log₂n per insert)
- Placed confirmation as its own designed state, not a derived list view
- Ranked list with genre/decade filter chips (minimal — filter-and-empty-state only, no saved views)
- TV titles are addable but show as ranking-disabled with a real, tappable "Unlock with FaveOff+" CTA that opens a "coming soon" sheet (no live transaction)
- Free movie-ranking cap enforced at 25, with its own capped-state screen using the same CTA/sheet pattern
- Taste Card render + working ShareLink export
- Full data model locked now: `Title.type`, optional genre, optional decade/year — so no future migration is needed even though filter UI and StoreKit are sequenced later
- Empty/loading/success/error states and VoiceOver labels on every reachable screen in this slice

**Cut list:** real StoreKit 2 purchase flow (sandbox config, transactions, restore-purchase) — explicitly the next slice, not deferred indefinitely; no tutorial/onboarding; no WidgetKit; no periodic recap cards; no saved/named sub-rank profiles; no drag-to-reorder; no live metadata/poster API in the core loop.

**Validation criteria:** fresh install, no network — add 3+ movies, complete bounded comparisons, reach a coherent ranked list and a legible, shareable Taste Card in under ~3 minutes; the same two titles compared twice never produce a contradictory order, including after relaunch; skip never corrupts the sort invariant; adding a TV title never pollutes movie ranking and never blocks the movie loop; the cap state appears predictably at exactly 25 ranked movies without blocking further adds; every screen in this slice shows correct empty/loading/success/error behavior. Failure on any of these is a defect in this slice, not a note for later polish.

# faveoff — Detailed Discussion

_Generated by the autonomous multi-agent orchestrator on 2026-07-08 12:24:56._

## Original Prompt

```
PORTFOLIO_CHILD_PROJECT: true
Parent portfolio: multi-app-exp7
Selected app slug: faveoff

Build this app as a working local-first SwiftUI MVP.

## Selected App Spec

# FaveOff

Build mode: **MVP build**.
Build priority: **4**.

## App Name

FaveOff

## Category

Entertainment / Taste Ranking

## One-Sentence Promise

Rank the movies and shows you've watched head-to-head, and FaveOff builds your real, personal top 10 — then turns it into a shareable taste card.

## Target User

Anyone who watches movies/shows and has opinions but finds star ratings meaningless and social-network movie apps like overhead — mass-market watchers, not cinephile completionists.

## Painful Moment Solved

Rating a movie 1-5 stars and realizing that number means nothing next to the 40 other movies you've also rated 4 stars — you genuinely don't know your own top 10.

## Why It Is Different

Instead of star ratings, shows two watched titles and asks which was better, building an Elo-style ranked list from a handful of quick head-to-head picks — far more decisive than star ratings, ending in a poster-grid 'Taste Card' built for sharing, with zero social graph or account required to get value.

## Why Users Would Pay

Free tier ranks movies only with a capped list length; FaveOff+ unlocks TV shows and music/albums as separate ranking tracks, unlimited list length, genre-filtered sub-rankings, and periodic 'Wrapped'-style recap cards.

## Subscription Model

Freemium, $3.99/mo or $24.99/yr.

## Local-First MVP Scope

SwiftData store for titles, comparisons, and computed rankings; ranking algorithm runs fully on-device; title/poster metadata lookup is the one network dependency, isolated behind a protocol.

## Future Backend Roadmap

Repository boundary allows CloudKit sync across devices and, later, an optional social taste-comparison layer without touching the core ranking engine.

## AR / Local ML / Local LLM Fit

None load-bearing; the hard technical capability is a proper Elo/merge-sort-style pairwise ranking engine, not ML.

## Design Direction

Cinematic dark-mode-first system — near-black backgrounds, marquee-red accent, bold condensed poster-style typography, poster artwork as the dominant visual element, film-reel-inspired transitions.

## App Store Positioning

Star ratings lie to you. FaveOff makes you pick, one match-up at a time, until your real top 10 falls out.

## Invalidation Criteria

If users abandon mid-ranking after only a handful of comparisons and never reach a shareable top-10, the comparison flow is too tedious and needs a faster convergence algorithm or smaller default list size.

## v1 Build Scope

Add titles (movies+TV), head-to-head ranking engine, ranked list view, Taste Card generation + ShareLink, FaveOff+ paywall with StoreKit.

## Core Workflows

- add a watched title
- head-to-head comparison rounds
- browse computed ranked list
- generate & share Taste Card (poster grid of top 10)
- filter rankings by genre/decade

## iOS-Native Features

- ShareLink for Taste Card
- ImageRenderer for poster-grid card generation
- WidgetKit small widget showing current #1

## Key Risks

- The one network touchpoint (title/poster lookup) must degrade gracefully offline (initials/color-block placeholder), never blocking the core ranking loop
- Comparison fatigue on large libraries — needs a smart pairing algorithm scaling ~n log n, not full round-robin n²

## Claude Design Handoff Prompt

Design a cinematic entertainment-ranking app called FaveOff: near-black dark-mode-first backgrounds, marquee-red accent, bold condensed poster-style typography, movie/show poster art as the dominant visual content, film-reel-inspired swipe/transition motion for head-to-head comparison screens, feels like a red-carpet awards show, not a spreadsheet.

## Parent Portfolio Prompt

Build 5 completely separate, production-ready iOS apps at the same time.

This is a multi-app PORTFOLIO request: multiple apps in one program. Each selected app becomes its own separate project — one folder per app, never bundled together.

Pick any 5 categories/concepts — no fixed assignment. Choose the 5 strongest MASS-MARKET ideas.

TOP PRIORITY — VIRAL, MASS-MARKET APPEAL (hard requirement):
- Every app must target a BROAD, mainstream audience. NO niche-community apps (no hobbyist tools, no professional verticals, no special-interest utilities). If your parents, your barista, and a college student wouldn't all instantly get it, it fails.
- Each app must have a built-in viral loop: something people naturally share, screenshot, send to friends, or compete over (shareable results/cards, streaks, challenges, comparisons, "send this to a friend" moments).
- The concept must be explainable in one sentence and demo-able in 10 seconds.
- Think the scale of habit trackers, photo tools, social utilities, everyday life tools, casual self-improvement, money, food, sleep, relationships, entertainment — things with tens of millions of potential users.

SECOND PRIORITY — DESIGN & UI/UX:
- World-class, distinctive visual identity per app: deliberate color system, type scale, spacing, iconography, motion language — not stock SwiftUI defaults.
- "Apple Design Award" caliber polish. Every screen considered: empty, loading, error states, transitions, haptics, spring animations.

THIRD PRIORITY — COMPLEXITY & DEPTH:

- Depth that rewards daily use — but never at the expense of instant mass-market clarity.

FOURTH PRIORITY — MONETIZATION:
- Clear, sustainable revenue path per app (subscription tiers, IAP, premium) that feels native to the product, never punitive. Free tier hooks users; paid tier is compelling.

General Requirements:
1. Each app goes from 0 to production-ready.
2. Each app must be unique, useful, and commercially viable, better than competitors in a meaningful way.
3. Local-first, architected so cloud support can be added later without rewriting.

UNIQUENESS (hard rule, non-negotiable):
- No app in this batch may be similar to another app in this batch.
- No app may be similar to anything already built in this workspace: Waylay, TrueScale, Provenance, Scope-Guard, Practice-Loop, Proof, ReturnWise, VerveCoach, CueKeeper, brinekeeper, chalkline, quietpilot, cistern, thicket, undersong, undertow, heddle, marginalia, skyglass, and the ~50 apps of prior batches (aarti-path, afterglow, backtimer, chapterly, cleanline, clearpath-access, crockwatch, crosswire, crux-cycle, curio, dayframe, dharma-trail, docket, flinch, heartline, kitbag, night-sky-logbook, pattern-pulse, pillcheck, referral-loop, risk-compass, rollcall, runway, scenario-ledger, shelf, sincerely, tabzero, talkthrough, tessella, throughline, trestle, triptally, umbra, wordsmith-atlas, and others).
- Produce differentiated concepts even if a category overlaps.

Bonus points (optional, only when integral): unique use of LLMs, AR, or ML.

Build rules:
- Run the five app efforts in parallel; keep phases separate per app.
- TIME MATTERS on this batch: reach consensus quickly in discussion phases (end phases early on agreement), but never skip the build, and never mark an app done unless a real, compiling Xcode project with full source exists in app_build. An app without a buildable Xcode project is a FAILURE.
- Save all phase discussions; combine each app's transcript into a .txt at the end.
- Recover cleanly from stalls; report only important milestones.

Output:

- /Users/pchordia/Documents/iOS-App-Factory
```
