# faveoff — App Features

_Generated by the autonomous multi-agent orchestrator on 2026-07-08 12:30:39._

## Original Prompt

```
PORTFOLIO_CHILD_PROJECT: true
Parent portfolio: multi-app-exp7
Selected app slug: faveoff

Build this app as a working local-first SwiftUI MVP.

## Selected App Spec

# FaveOff

Build mode: **MVP build**.
Build priority: **4**.

## App Name

FaveOff

## Category

Entertainment / Taste Ranking

## One-Sentence Promise

Rank the movies and shows you've watched head-to-head, and FaveOff builds your real, personal top 10 — then turns it into a shareable taste card.

## Target User

Anyone who watches movies/shows and has opinions but finds star ratings meaningless and social-network movie apps like overhead — mass-market watchers, not cinephile completionists.

## Painful Moment Solved

Rating a movie 1-5 stars and realizing that number means nothing next to the 40 other movies you've also rated 4 stars — you genuinely don't know your own top 10.

## Why It Is Different

Instead of star ratings, shows two watched titles and asks which was better, building an Elo-style ranked list from a handful of quick head-to-head picks — far more decisive than star ratings, ending in a poster-grid 'Taste Card' built for sharing, with zero social graph or account required to get value.

## Why Users Would Pay

Free tier ranks movies only with a capped list length; FaveOff+ unlocks TV shows and music/albums as separate ranking tracks, unlimited list length, genre-filtered sub-rankings, and periodic 'Wrapped'-style recap cards.

## Subscription Model

Freemium, $3.99/mo or $24.99/yr.

## Local-First MVP Scope

SwiftData store for titles, comparisons, and computed rankings; ranking algorithm runs fully on-device; title/poster metadata lookup is the one network dependency, isolated behind a protocol.

## Future Backend Roadmap

Repository boundary allows CloudKit sync across devices and, later, an optional social taste-comparison layer without touching the core ranking engine.

## AR / Local ML / Local LLM Fit

None load-bearing; the hard technical capability is a proper Elo/merge-sort-style pairwise ranking engine, not ML.

## Design Direction

Cinematic dark-mode-first system — near-black backgrounds, marquee-red accent, bold condensed poster-style typography, poster artwork as the dominant visual element, film-reel-inspired transitions.

## App Store Positioning

Star ratings lie to you. FaveOff makes you pick, one match-up at a time, until your real top 10 falls out.

## Invalidation Criteria

If users abandon mid-ranking after only a handful of comparisons and never reach a shareable top-10, the comparison flow is too tedious and needs a faster convergence algorithm or smaller default list size.

## v1 Build Scope

Add titles (movies+TV), head-to-head ranking engine, ranked list view, Taste Card generation + ShareLink, FaveOff+ paywall with StoreKit.

## Core Workflows

- add a watched title
- head-to-head comparison rounds
- browse computed ranked list
- generate & share Taste Card (poster grid of top 10)
- filter rankings by genre/decade

## iOS-Native Features

- ShareLink for Taste Card
- ImageRenderer for poster-grid card generation
- WidgetKit small widget showing current #1

## Key Risks

- The one network touchpoint (title/poster lookup) must degrade gracefully offline (initials/color-block placeholder), never blocking the core ranking loop
- Comparison fatigue on large libraries — needs a smart pairing algorithm scaling ~n log n, not full round-robin n²

## Claude Design Handoff Prompt

Design a cinematic entertainment-ranking app called FaveOff: near-black dark-mode-first backgrounds, marquee-red accent, bold condensed poster-style typography, movie/show poster art as the dominant visual content, film-reel-inspired swipe/transition motion for head-to-head comparison screens, feels like a red-carpet awards show, not a spreadsheet.

## Parent Portfolio Prompt

Build 5 completely separate, production-ready iOS apps at the same time.

This is a multi-app PORTFOLIO request: multiple apps in one program. Each selected app becomes its own separate project — one folder per app, never bundled together.

Pick any 5 categories/concepts — no fixed assignment. Choose the 5 strongest MASS-MARKET ideas.

TOP PRIORITY — VIRAL, MASS-MARKET APPEAL (hard requirement):
- Every app must target a BROAD, mainstream audience. NO niche-community apps (no hobbyist tools, no professional verticals, no special-interest utilities). If your parents, your barista, and a college student wouldn't all instantly get it, it fails.
- Each app must have a built-in viral loop: something people naturally share, screenshot, send to friends, or compete over (shareable results/cards, streaks, challenges, comparisons, "send this to a friend" moments).
- The concept must be explainable in one sentence and demo-able in 10 seconds.
- Think the scale of habit trackers, photo tools, social utilities, everyday life tools, casual self-improvement, money, food, sleep, relationships, entertainment — things with tens of millions of potential users.

SECOND PRIORITY — DESIGN & UI/UX:
- World-class, distinctive visual identity per app: deliberate color system, type scale, spacing, iconography, motion language — not stock SwiftUI defaults.
- "Apple Design Award" caliber polish. Every screen considered: empty, loading, error states, transitions, haptics, spring animations.
- Dark mode, Dynamic Type, accessibility (VoiceOver, 44pt targets, WCAG AA contrast).
- Each of the 5 apps must look clearly DIFFERENT from the other four — five distinct design directions.
- Documented design system per app (tokens, components, states); built UI must match it.

THIRD PRIORITY — COMPLEXITY & DEPTH:
- Genuinely substantial apps — interconnected features, real domain data model, non-trivial logic, offline persistence with sync-later architecture, robust settings, import/export, and at least one hard technical capability done well.
- Depth that rewards daily use — but never at the expense of instant mass-market clarity.

FOURTH PRIORITY — MONETIZATION:
- Clear, sustainable revenue path per app (subscription tiers, IAP, premium) that feels native to the product, never punitive. Free tier hooks users; paid tier is compelling.

General Requirements:
1. Each app goes from 0 to production-ready.
2. Each app must be unique, useful, and commercially viable, better than competitors in a meaningful way.
3. Local-first, architected so cloud support can be added later without rewriting.

UNIQUENESS (hard rule, non-negotiable):
- No app in this batch may be similar to another app in this batch.
- No app may be similar to anything already built in this workspace: Waylay, TrueScale, Provenance, Scope-Guard, Practice-Loop, Proof, ReturnWise, VerveCoach, CueKeeper, brinekeeper, chalkline, quietpilot, cistern, thicket, undersong, undertow, heddle, marginalia, skyglass, and the ~50 apps of prior batches (aarti-path, afterglow, backtimer, chapterly, cleanline, clearpath-access, crockwatch, crosswire, crux-cycle, curio, dayframe, dharma-trail, docket, flinch, heartline, kitbag, night-sky-logbook, pattern-pulse, pillcheck, referral-loop, risk-compass, rollcall, runway, scenario-ledger, shelf, sincerely, tabzero, talkthrough, tessella, throughline, trestle, triptally, umbra, wordsmith-atlas, and others).
- Produce differentiated concepts even if a category overlaps.

Bonus points (optional, only when integral): unique use of LLMs, AR, or ML.

Build rules:
- Run the five app efforts in parallel; keep phases separate per app.
- TIME MATTERS on this batch: reach consensus quickly in discussion phases (end phases early on agreement), but never skip the build, and never mark an app done unless a real, compiling Xcode project with full source exists in app_build. An app without a buildable Xcode project is a FAILURE.
- Save all phase discussions; combine each app's transcript into a .txt at the end.
- Recover cleanly from stalls; report only important milestones.

Output:
- One folder per app in the output directory — never one folder containing all apps.
Output directory:
- /Users/pchordia/Documents/iOS-App-Factory

Now generate at least 10 candidate ideas, judge them primarily on mass-market viral potential, choose the best 5, and build all 5 to production quality with exceptional UI/UX.
```

## Phase Purpose

Enumerate and prioritize the full feature set with an explicit scoring / MoSCoW model and clear in-scope vs out-of-scope lines.

## Transcript


### Round 1


**Codex (Design Lead · Skeptic) — Round 1**

`M`ust / `S`hould / `C`ould / `W`on’t:

- `M` Core product loop (`Add title -> pairwise compare -> Placed confirmation -> ranked list`)  
  Why: everything else in the app exists to support this loop and this is the only way users reach the Taste Card promise quickly.  
  In-scope: manual add with title + type + optional metadata, immediate insertion state, no required tutorial, no network block.  
  Out-of-scope: background metadata fetching that can pause or defer ranking.

- `M` Ranked insertion engine implemented as persistent binary-search insertion with explicit skip contract  
  Why: this is the “science” of the app and the anti-fatigue core mechanic.  
  Rule to preserve: log₂(n) comparisons bound, deterministic skip fallback, sort order invariant never breaks.  
  In-scope: persisted `lo/hi/lastCompared/skipBudget` for crash/interruption recovery.  
  Out-of-scope: Elo, round-robin, or full sort passes for ranking.

- `M` Placed confirmation state as a reward moment (not a passive list update)  
  Why: first use experience depends on feeling the result, not just entering data.  
  In-scope: “#N, above X, below Y” style feedback with honest fallback copy when insertion is uncertain.  
  Out-of-scope: delayed animation-only confirmation after list refresh.

- `M` Local persistence + relaunch recovery across app terminations  
  Why: trust sink if entered items disappear or reorder unpredictably.  
  In-scope: SwiftData repo, schema includes `titleType`, optional `year`, fixed single genre, rankingState; resume in-progress insertion exactly from persisted bounds.

- `M` TV track boundary + free movie cap boundary from day one  
  Why: this is a locked monetization/experience contract, not a late bolt-on rule.  
  In-scope: TV can be added/logged, but ranking and cards blocked until upgrade; movie ranking capped at exactly 25 ranked items for free; visible “Unlock with FaveOff+” CTA.  
  Out-of-scope: softening the boundary “to reduce friction” in this phase.

- `M` Real paywall behavior (sandbox-only StoreKit 2 flow)  
  Why: production-readiness lock and prevents hidden scope drift.  
  In-scope: local `.storekit`, two products, purchase/restoration, `Transaction.currentEntitlements`, failure states isolated to purchase sheet, ranking remains usable regardless of network or failed checkout.  
  Out-of-scope: fake conversion UI that implies full transaction logic but does nothing.

- `M` Ranked list + genre/decade filter chips in browse  
  Why: it is a core workflow in spec and a low-risk test of data-model integrity.  
  In-scope: fixed empty/no-results states and quick reset.  
  Out-of-scope: saved/named sub-rank profiles.

- `M` Taste Card generation and sharing pipeline  
  Why: this is the viral loop and must work without logins.  
  In-scope: `ImageRenderer` → branded poster-grid card → `ShareLink` sharing path.  
  Out-of-scope: custom photo-save/export flows (rely on ShareLink’s native path).

- `S` Optional metadata enrichment path via protocol  
  Why: offline-safe loop is already complete; enrichment improves UX if available.  
  In-scope: offline stub as default, optional provider behind protocol, no ranking dependency.  
  Out-of-scope: hard dependency on lookup API, no spinner that blocks ranking.

- `S` Delete + archive behavior  
  Why: needed for maintenance and deletion must not crash or corrupt order.  
  In-scope: delete a ranked title safely; comparisons soft-archived/ignored; active compare cancellation with explicit copy.  
  Out-of-scope: deep graph rewrite or tombstone UX.

- `S` Accessibility/comparative readability hardening pass  
  Why: we cannot ship a cinematic theme that breaks reading/contrast.  
  In-scope: VoiceOver labels for every action target, 44pt min, contrast targets logged against body and large text.  
  Out-of-scope: custom gesture-only discovery and ambiguous icon-only controls.

- `C` Brand-forward visual polish (film-reel transition language, motion, haptic micro-feedback)  
  Why: important but secondary to core loop reliability.  
  In-scope: consistent theme tokens and restrained motion that reinforces compare reward.  
  Out-of-scope: full “cinema trailer” treatment that slows core flow.

- `C` “I already watched it” import/CSV history  
  Why: useful but not required for proof of concept trust and adds import-risk.  
  Out-of-scope for now.

- `W` WidgetKit  
  Why: explicitly first-to-cut under pressure and not required to validate first-use loop.  
  Out-of-scope.

- `W` Manual drag-to-reorder  
  Why: destroys premise of comparison-driven trust and invites gaming.  
  Out-of-scope.

- `W` Tutorial/onboarding screens  
  Why: if this is needed, the comparison screen needs simplification instead.  
  Out-of-scope.

- `W` Social feed/graph, push notifications, backups/cloud sync, analytics, custom photo permission flows  
  Why: non-essential and violates local-first/no-network-first posture.  
  Out-of-scope.

Biggest risk to this direction: overfitting the flow with too many boundary states (cap, TV lock, paywall, skip fallback, filters, loading/error states) can make the first session feel like policy screens instead of a fun loop. The way to defend against that is to keep comparison and placement the dominant path: any non-core screen must be dismissible and non-blocking; ranking never waits on any paid or metadata condition.

Acceptance criteria per must-have:
- A fresh no-network install can complete add → compare → placed confirmation → ranked list → share card in one run with clear empty/first-title states and no crash.
- New title insertion uses at most `ceil(log2(n+1))` real comparisons; if two consecutive skips occur, insertion terminates deterministically within the persisted window with copy that explains confidence level.
- Order is stable across repeated runs: no pair in ranked list ever inverts after relaunch.
- TV add while free and cap reached do not block movie flow; both boundaries are surfaced with one-tap upgrade CTA.
- App termination at any point in insertion, relaunch, and resume lands either back to same pending pair or correctly persisted ranked state.
- Share card image renders at thumbnail preview size with readable brand mark and top-10 posters.
- StoreKit purchase errors or slow checkout never block add/compare/list behavior; entitlements are reconciled on launch and Restore Purchases is present.
- Every reachable screen has empty/loading/success/error affordances and accessible labels.

**Claude (QA & Risk · Pragmatist) — Round 1**

Looking at everything locked across the last four phases, my job this round is to turn all of that into a feature list that's actually buildable, and to be the one voice asking "will this really fit, and how would we know if it didn't." So let me draft what I think must-have vs should/could vs won't looks like, with acceptance criteria tight enough that a QA pass could actually fail them.

Must-haves, each with the user story and a falsifiable acceptance test, not a vibe: (1) Add a title — as a user I can add a movie or TV title with just a name and type in under 10 seconds, optional year/genre, no network required; acceptance: with airplane mode on, adding a title succeeds, shows placeholder initials/color-block art, and the title appears in the correct unranked/pending state immediately. (2) Head-to-head comparison with skip — as a user comparing a new title I see one pairwise choice at a time bounded to ceil(log2(n+1)) comparisons, with a skip option that terminates after two consecutive skips and places the title at the nearest window edge; acceptance: instrument a test that adds a 25th title and skips the first two comparisons — it must land at the bottom with the specific "come back and compare it for a more precise spot" copy, not a generic label, and must never produce a placement that contradicts a previously confirmed pairwise result. (3) Placed confirmation screen — as a user I see exactly where my title landed and what it's adjacent to; acceptance: 0-item and 1-item cold starts produce coherent copy ("first one in..."), never "#1 of 1, ahead of nothing." (4) Ranked list with genre/decade filter chips — acceptance: filters read from the `year`/genre fields already on the schema, no-match state shows a nudge not a blank screen. (5) TV-addable-but-ranking-disabled with a real tappable upgrade CTA — acceptance: adding a TV title never blocks or alters movie ranking state, and the ranking button on a TV title is visibly disabled with explanatory text, not just missing. (6) 25-item free movie cap with its own capped-state screen — acceptance: the cap state appears exactly at the 25th ranked movie, further adds still succeed, only new ranking beyond 25 is blocked. (7) Taste Card generation + ShareLink — acceptance: card renders and is legible at iMessage-thumbnail size with a persistent brand mark, generated via ImageRenderer as a static image so later title deletion can't corrupt an already-shared card. (8) Real sandboxed StoreKit 2 purchase flow — acceptance: local `.storekit` config, both products purchasable in sandbox, `Transaction.currentEntitlements` reconciled on launch, Restore Purchases visible, and a forced network failure during purchase must leave the ranking loop completely unaffected — that's a real test case, not a hope. (9) Crash/interruption-safe persistence — acceptance: kill the app mid-insertion (mid-comparison), relaunch, and the in-flight insertion resumes from persisted `lo`/`hi`/`lastComparedIndex`, never silently drops the title or auto-ranks it without a real comparison or honest skip-fallback. (10) Accessibility as a gate — acceptance: measured contrast ratio ≥4.5:1 body / ≥3:1 large-bold across the full Dynamic Type range on every comparison and Placed screen, checked with an actual contrast tool, not eyeballed.

My biggest worry with this list as stated is that items 8 and 9 together are a lot of unglamorous plumbing — StoreKit sandbox config plus crash-safe insertion-state persistence — competing for build time against the thing that actually has to feel great, which is the comparison-to-Placed emotional beat. If I had to name the single feature most likely to get silently under-built under time pressure, it's not StoreKit (already flagged as non-cuttable) — it's the crash-recovery persistence, because it's invisible when it works and only shows up as a bug report later. I want that named explicitly as a must-have with its own acceptance test in the final output, not folded as a sub-bullet of "the data model," because it's exactly the kind of thing that's easy to skip when you're testing happy paths.

For should/could: the non-blocking "similar title already exists" duplicate nudge is a could — cheap, additive, not required for the core loop to prove itself, cut first if time is short. Filter-chip persistence (remembering the last filter you had selected) is a could, not a should. A more precise per-title "confidence" indicator (e.g., showing how many real comparisons a title has actually been through) is a should — it's directly in service of the trust concern the whole spec is worried about, cheap to add as a small label, but not required to hit the acceptance bar above, so it can slip to should without breaking the promise.

Won't-build, and I want these written down explicitly so nobody quietly re-adds them mid-build: WidgetKit (still first-cut-under-pressure per the locked priority order), any tutorial/onboarding screen, manual drag-to-reorder, social graph/feed, periodic recap cards, saved/named sub-rank profiles, music/album track, live metadata/poster API as a load-bearing dependency, push notifications, a custom Photos-save button, data export/backup beyond the Taste Card image, full Ford-Johnson merge-insertion, classic Elo, and full round-robin n² comparisons. None of these support the "add → compare → Placed → share" promise directly enough to justify the build risk right now, and several of them (widget, recap cards) are explicitly the kind of feature that would require new plumbing (App Group containers, scheduled generation) disproportionate to their value in a first build.

The one thing I want flagged as a real risk to the list as a whole: item 8 (real StoreKit) and item 2 (bounded comparison engine with a correct skip-termination rule) are, by a wide margin, the two hardest and most bug-prone pieces here, and they have almost nothing to do with each other technically — meaning there's no way to "reuse" effort between them, they're two fully separate hard problems inside one MVP. If build time gets tight, my acceptance criteria above should be treated as the actual go/no-go bar, not "well, it mostly works" — a StoreKit flow that silently fails to reconcile entitlements on relaunch, or a skip rule that doesn't terminate deterministically, are both launch-blocking bugs, not polish notes, and I'd rather this phase say that explicitly than leave it implied.

**Coordinator (Claude) — decision after round 1**

CONSENSUS: NO

Here's where things stand after round 1. Codex and Claude actually agree on almost the entire shape of the must-have list: the core add→compare→Placed→ranked-list loop, the bounded binary-search insertion engine with the deterministic skip rule, crash/relaunch-safe persistence of the in-flight insertion state, the TV-addable-but-ranking-disabled boundary, the 25-item free movie cap, the real sandboxed StoreKit 2 flow, filter chips on the ranked list, and Taste Card + ShareLink are all Must, with basically the same acceptance-test flavor (skip-twice-on-a-large-list as a named test case, contrast/Dynamic Type checks, entitlement reconciliation on launch, etc.). The won't-build list also lines up closely — widget, tutorial screens, drag-to-reorder, social graph, recap cards, push notifications, custom photo-save, and the wrong-algorithm options (Elo, Ford-Johnson, round-robin) are all out for both.

Two real gaps between them that round 2 needs to close:

1. **Accessibility's priority tier** — Codex filed the "accessibility/contrast hardening pass" as a **Should**, treating it as important-but-secondary polish. Claude treats it as a **Must** with its own falsifiable acceptance test (measured ≥4.5:1/≥3:1 contrast across Dynamic Type, checked with an actual tool). Given that earlier phases already locked numeric WCAG targets as a launch-blocking gate, this looks like it should resolve toward Claude's framing, but Codex hasn't been asked to defend or concede the Should placement yet.

2. **Delete/archive behavior's status** — Codex lists delete+archive as a Should. Claude's must-have list doesn't mention deletion at all, so it's unclear if Claude agrees it's a Should, thinks it's a Must, or just didn't get to it. This needs an explicit call, since deletion mid-comparison was already locked as required behavior in the prior detailed_discussion phase.

There's also a smaller unresolved thread: Codex has "optional metadata enrichment via protocol" as a Should, which Claude's list doesn't address — probably fine to fold in as agreed, but it should get an explicit nod next round rather than being silently assumed.

Next round should get Codex and Claude to explicitly settle accessibility's tier (Must vs. Should) and pin down where deletion lands, then this phase is basically done — everything else already reads like convergence, not a fight.

### Round 2


**Codex (Design Lead · Skeptic) — Round 2**

I want to push back on one thing from our current framing before we lock this: if we keep treating accessibility as a “should,” we’re contradicting our own production bar. Claude already anchored this in earlier notes with hard ratios, and the design direction here is intentionally high-contrast-sensitive (near-black + marque-red). If we don't force measured WCAG checks on every reachable comparison/placed screen, the exact first-session emotional promise can disappear in practice for bigger text users because the rank numbers and CTA labels become unreadable while the flow “looks fine” in screenshot reviews. So I’m explicitly putting accessibility contrast + VoiceOver labels + 44pt targets in MUST, not Should.

For flow priority, I’m mostly aligned with both you and Claude on keeping the app narrow: add → compare → placed → ranked list → share. But the hidden landmine is state soup. If we claim this is a fast ritual but then let edge paths throw users into ambiguous states, we lose that promise. So yes to the TV boundary and 25-item cap now; but let’s collapse their presentation into the least invasive patterns: one clearly disabled action row and one non-blocking capped banner. No modal chains on every action.

I disagree with any version of queueing where users can see a stack of pending titles before ranking. That’s functionally defensible, but it’s UX debt for this slice. Keep strict single-active insertion, but persist it aggressively so crash/background kill is recoverable. That means `rankingState` with `inProgress` and persisted `lo/hi/lastCompared/skipBudget` is not optional.

On deletion, I think this one is not Should; it’s Must. We already know we can kill an in-flight insertion by deleting that title, and that’s an interaction with ranking integrity, not cleanup nicety. If we can’t tolerate that failure mode, we’re not shipping a stable product. So deletion of ranked items must remove it cleanly, soft-ignore related comparison refs, and show explicit "title removed, comparison canceled" when applicable. No crashes, no dangling refs.

On store flow, I’m with Codex’s tightened view: real StoreKit 2 in this build. The risk argument “later is fine” sounds efficient, but it violates the phase contract trail where StoreKit sandbox truth was explicitly in-scope and would otherwise silently regress here. We can keep it contained so it never touches ranking, but if someone buys, relaunch reconciliation and Restore must be real. I’m not arguing for a “full subscription dashboard”; just a productiony transaction sheet with error/pending paths.

What I still think is a risky shortcut is calling genre/decade too small while locking a clean schema. That is exactly the kind of feature that either works or exposes bug surfaces fast. We should keep genre/decade chips, but only the minimal filter pass: one-pass enum genre + derived decade from year and a no-results nudge. Anything like saved profiles can stay could.

I want one extra UX hardening in the placed screen itself: skip-on-large-list fallback copy must be brutally honest, and specifically not “placed by quick-pass.” We already have the test case in spirit; I want it explicit in acceptance: skip twice as first moves on a long list must deterministically place near window edge and label “added to the bottom for now; compare later to refine.” If we don’t frame it this way, users interpret it as arbitrary guesswork and abandonment risk doubles.

So my stance for this phase: mandatory features are still exactly the core promise + hard reliability boundaries (insert engine, skip contract, cap + TV lock, ShareCard, StoreKit, crash-safe resume, delete safety, and accessibility gates). Shoulds are optional polish that helps trust and flow (duplicate nudge, metadata enrichment provider, micro-haptics). Coulds are later iterations (queue depth indicator for rapid adds, recap cards, named saved sub-rank views). Won’ts stay unchanged: widget, recap cards, social graph, no tutorial, drag reorder, round-robin/Elo, forced re-comparisons.

I’d like the same acceptance framing to be explicit per must-have: bounded-comparison proof, deterministic skip test on 25-item list, relaunch resume, entitlement failure isolation, shared-card render at thumbnail legibility, and WCAG checks across Dynamic Type. If any one of those fails, this feature bucket is not done, not “good enough to polish later.”

**Claude (QA & Risk · Pragmatist) — Round 2**

Codex, your list is genuinely close to mine and I don't think we need another round after this one — but let me actually settle the two things the coordinator called out instead of hand-waving past them.

On accessibility: you filed it as a Should — "brand-forward visual polish... secondary to core loop reliability" is a separate bullet from your accessibility one, so I don't think you're saying accessibility itself is cosmetic, but the tier placement still reads that way, and I want to push back on it specifically. This isn't a "hardening pass" you bolt on after the screens exist — it's a gate that was already locked with actual numbers in detailed_discussion (≥4.5:1 body, ≥3:1 large/bold, checked across the full Dynamic Type range, "before any comparison or Placed screen is considered done"). That phrase "considered done" is doing real work: it means a comparison screen that hasn't been contrast-checked isn't a finished must-have feature, it's an unfinished one wearing a done label. If we file it as Should, the natural build-order consequence is that it gets checked after the two screens that matter most (Compare and Placed) are already "working," which is exactly backwards — those are the specific screens the condensed marquee-red-on-black type risk was named against from the very first product-research phase. My test for Must vs Should has always been "if this is missing, is the corresponding Must-have actually incomplete" — and here, yes: a Compare screen that fails WCAG AA at large Dynamic Type sizes hasn't actually delivered on must-have #2, it's just delivered a version of it that's broken for a chunk of real users. So I want it explicitly folded into the acceptance criteria of the comparison and Placed-state must-haves themselves, not left as a freestanding Should that could get deprioritized under time pressure. Practically: same list of screens, just move the line from "S" to attached-acceptance-criteria-on-the-relevant-Musts.

On deletion — you're right to notice I skipped it, and I want to be straight about why: I don't actually think it's a Should, but I also don't think it deserves a fully separate line item competing for build attention against the ten things I listed. Here's my read: deletion mid-active-comparison was locked as required behavior in detailed_discussion specifically because it's a crash-adjacent safety property, not a feature — "title deleted while displayed in a live comparison screen: cancel cleanly, return to list, no crash" is the same category of thing as my must-have #9 (crash-safe persistence), just triggered by a user action instead of an OS kill. If we ship #9 properly — meaning insertion state is always derived from persisted SwiftData truth rather than assumed to still exist in memory — then handling "the title I'm mid-compare on just got deleted" is largely the same code path as handling "the app got killed mid-compare," because both are really "what happens when the thing my insertion referenced is gone." So my actual position: fold basic delete/archive (no crash, no dangling references, comparisons soft-archived, mid-compare cancellation with explicit copy) into must-have #9's acceptance criteria rather than giving it its own bullet — but call out explicitly that a delete button has to exist somewhere in the UI for this to even be testable, since right now none of our screens mention one. If Codex, you want it as its own separate Should because you think the affordance itself (where does delete live in the UI, swipe-to-delete vs. a detail-screen button) is a real design decision that needs its own space rather than riding along as a side effect of persistence — I'd actually hear that, since "where does delete live" is a UI surface question, distinct from "does deletion corrupt state," which is the crash-safety question. So maybe the cleanest split is: delete-safety (no crash, no corruption) rides as acceptance criteria on must-have #9, and delete-as-a-discoverable-UI-affordance stays a Should, since a user not being able to find the delete button is annoying but not trust-destroying the way a crash or silent data loss is.

One more thing I want to flag before we call this done, because Codex's framing of the "biggest risk" is worth building on rather than just nodding at: you said the risk is the first session feeling like "policy screens instead of a fun loop," and your fix is "any non-core screen must be dismissible and non-blocking." I'd tighten that into something testable rather than a design principle, because "must be dismissible" is exactly the kind of requirement that sounds locked in a discussion phase and then quietly doesn't get verified in build. Concrete version: in a first session with a fresh install, count the number of taps between "I just added my 3rd movie" and "I'm looking at my ranked list of 3" — if the TV-lock explainer, the cap warning, or the paywall CTA ever appears in that path unprompted (i.e., the user didn't tap something that leads to them), that's a failure, full stop, because none of those three things should ever be reachable before someone has actually hit the cap, added a TV title, or tapped upgrade. That's the difference between "policy screens exist" (fine, they have to) and "policy screens get in the way" (not fine, and exactly what you're worried about) — and it gives build/QA an actual thing to check instead of a vibe to avoid.

With those two folded in — accessibility's numeric gate attached directly to the Compare/Placed must-haves rather than living as a separate Should, and delete-safety folded into the persistence must-have's acceptance criteria while UI discoverability of delete stays a Should — I think our lists actually converge completely. Everything else, the Must/Should/Could/Won't buckets, and the acceptance tests, are the same list stated in slightly different words.

**Coordinator (Claude) — decision after round 2**

CONSENSUS: YES

They've closed both gaps the last round left open. Codex flipped accessibility (contrast ratios, VoiceOver, 44pt targets) from Should to Must, agreeing with Claude that this was already locked with real numbers in an earlier phase and can't be treated as a later hardening pass. On deletion, Codex wanted it as its own Must bullet; Claude's counter — fold delete-safety (no crash, no corruption, clean cancellation) into the crash-safe-persistence must-have's acceptance criteria since they're really the same "what happens when the thing my insertion referenced disappears" problem, while keeping "where the delete button actually lives in the UI" as a separate Should — is a packaging difference, not a substance disagreement; both agree deletion must never crash or corrupt state, they just organize it differently. Nothing else moved: the core loop, the insertion engine, the cap/TV boundary, StoreKit, and the Taste Card are Must for both, and the won't-build list hasn't been touched by either side in two rounds. Claude's added "count the taps" test for whether policy screens (TV lock, cap, paywall) ever appear unprompted is a concrete tightening of a risk Codex already named, not a new fight.

## Final Output

**Must-have features** (each with its user story and falsifiable acceptance test):

1. **Add a title** — as a user I can add a movie or TV title with just name + type in under 10 seconds, optional year/genre, no network required. *Acceptance:* with airplane mode on, adding a title succeeds, shows placeholder initials/color-block art, and appears in the correct unranked/pending state immediately.

2. **Head-to-head comparison with skip** — as a user placing a new title I see one pairwise choice at a time, bounded to `ceil(log2(n+1))` comparisons, with a skip option that terminates deterministically after two consecutive skips. *Acceptance:* adding a 25th title and skipping the first two comparisons lands it at the bottom with the exact non-generic copy ("added to the bottom for now — compare later to refine"), never a vague "quick-pass" label, and never contradicts a previously confirmed pairwise result.

3. **Placed confirmation screen** — as a user I see exactly where my title landed and what it's adjacent to, as a designed reward moment, not a passive list refresh. *Acceptance:* 0-item and 1-item cold starts produce coherent copy ("first one in..."), never a broken "#1 of 1, ahead of nothing" string.

4. **Ranked list with genre/decade filter chips** — minimal, one-pass filtering off the existing `year`/genre schema fields. *Acceptance:* no-match state shows a nudge, not a blank screen; filters never block or gate the base ranked list.

5. **TV-addable-but-ranking-disabled boundary** — TV titles can be added/logged for free but show a visibly disabled ranking action with a real "Unlock with FaveOff+" CTA. *Acceptance:* adding a TV title never blocks or alters movie ranking state; the disabled row is one clean, non-modal explainer, not a forced interruption.

6. **25-item free movie ranking cap** — enforced with its own non-blocking capped state. *Acceptance:* the cap state appears exactly at the 25th ranked movie; further title adds still succeed; only ranking beyond 25 is blocked.

7. **Taste Card generation + ShareLink** — `ImageRenderer` → branded poster-grid card → native share sheet. *Acceptance:* card is legible at iMessage-thumbnail size with a persistent brand mark; rendered as a static image so later title deletion can never corrupt an already-shared card.

8. **Real sandboxed StoreKit 2 purchase flow** — local `.storekit` config, both subscription products, real buy/pending/failed states, `Transaction.currentEntitlements` reconciliation on launch, visible Restore Purchases. *Acceptance:* a forced network failure or slow checkout stays fully contained to the purchase sheet and leaves the ranking loop completely unaffected — a real test case, not an assumption.

9. **Crash/interruption-safe persistence (including delete-safety)** — insertion state (`rankingState`, persisted `lo`/`hi`/`lastComparedIndex`/`skipBudget`) is always derived from SwiftData truth, never assumed in-memory. This must-have's acceptance criteria explicitly cover deletion, since it's the same failure category (the thing an in-flight insertion referenced is gone): deleting a title mid-active-comparison cancels that insertion cleanly, returns to the list with explicit "title removed, comparison canceled" copy, no crash, no dangling comparison references. *Acceptance:* kill the app mid-insertion, relaunch, and it resumes from persisted bounds exactly; delete a title while it's on-screen in a live comparison and the app recovers cleanly instead of crashing.

10. **Accessibility as a build gate on the Compare and Placed screens specifically** — measured contrast ≥4.5:1 body-weight / ≥3:1 large-bold text across the full Dynamic Type range, VoiceOver labels on every actionable element, 44pt touch targets. *Acceptance:* a Compare or Placed screen that hasn't been contrast-checked isn't considered a finished must-have — it's an incomplete one. This is folded directly into must-haves #2 and #3's definition of done, not a freestanding polish pass.

**Additional testable acceptance framing that applies across the whole must-have list:** count the taps between "user just added their 3rd movie" and "user is looking at their ranked list of 3" — if the TV-lock explainer, cap warning, or paywall CTA appears anywhere in that path unprompted (without the user tapping something that leads there), that's a failure. Policy/boundary screens are allowed to exist; they're not allowed to intrude on a path nobody asked to be shown.

**Should-have:** metadata enrichment via an optional protocol-backed provider (offline stub remains the default, never load-bearing); per-title "confidence" indicator showing how many real comparisons it's actually been through; delete-as-a-discoverable-UI-affordance (where the delete action actually lives — swipe, detail screen, etc. — as opposed to delete-safety itself, which is a Must); non-blocking "similar title already exists" duplicate nudge; light micro-haptics on comparison taps.

**Could-have:** brand-forward visual/motion polish (film-reel transitions) beyond the restrained baseline; filter-selection persistence across sessions; queue-depth indicator for rapid multi-add (explicitly deferred, separable from the crash-safety persistence which is already a Must); CSV/history import.

**Won't-build:** WidgetKit, tutorial/onboarding screens, manual drag-to-reorder, social graph/feed, periodic recap cards, saved/named sub-rank profiles, music/album ranking track, live metadata/poster API as a load-bearing dependency, push notifications, custom Photos-save button, data export/backup beyond the Taste Card image, classic Elo, full Ford-Johnson merge-insertion, full round-robin n² comparisons, forced re-comparisons.

**Quality Gate (Claude) — after round 2**

QUALITY: PASS

## Feedback
This output clears the bar. All four required elements are present and concrete: a 10-item must-have list each with a user story and a falsifiable acceptance test (not vague polish language — e.g. "skip twice on a 25th title lands at the bottom with exact non-generic copy," "cap state appears exactly at the 25th ranked movie," "forced network failure during purchase stays contained to the purchase sheet"), a should/could list with clear reasoning for why each item is deferrable, and a won't-build list that matches every non-goal locked in prior phases without drift.

The two real gaps the round-1 coordinator flagged are both resolved with actual reasoning rather than asserted: accessibility moves from Should to Must with a defensible argument (the WCAG numbers were already locked in `detailed_discussion` as a "before any screen is considered done" gate, so filing it as a separate Should would let Compare/Placed ship unchecked) and is folded directly into must-haves #2/#3's definition of done rather than left as a freestanding item that could slip. Deletion is resolved as a packaging decision, not a substance disagreement — delete-safety (no crash/corruption) rides on the crash-recovery must-have since they're the same "referenced thing disappeared" failure mode, while UI discoverability of the delete affordance is correctly separated out as a Should. Both speakers explicitly reasoned through this rather than one side just conceding.

The output stays fully consistent with everything locked in `prompt_contract`, `product_research`, `next_steps_small`, and `detailed_discussion` — binary-search insertion (not Ford-Johnson/Elo/round-robin), the deterministic 2-skip termination rule with honest fallback copy, TV-addable-but-ranking-disabled, the 25-item cap, real sandboxed StoreKit 2 (not a "coming soon" stub), and widget/tutorial/drag-reorder/social-graph all correctly in Won't. The added "count the taps" acceptance framing is a genuine tightening of Codex's own stated risk (policy screens becoming intrusive) into something a QA pass can actually check. This is a usable, buildable artifact for the next phase. No repair needed.

See the coordinator's message above.

On deletion, Codex wanted it as its own Must bullet; Claude's counter — fold delete-safety (no crash, no corruption, clean cancellation) into the crash-safe-persistence must-have's acceptance criteria since they're really the same "what happens when the thing my insertion referenced disappears" problem, while keeping "where the delete button actually lives in the UI" as a separate Should — is a packaging difference, not a substance disagreement; both agree deletion must never crash or corrupt state, they just organize it differently. Nothing else moved: the core loop, the insertion engine, the cap/TV boundary, StoreKit, and the Taste Card are Must for both, and the won't-build list hasn't been touched by either side in two rounds. ## Final Output

**Must-have features** (each with its user story and falsifiable acceptance test):

1. **Add a title** — as a user I can add a movie or TV title with just name + type in under 10 seconds, optional year/genre, no network required. *Acceptance:* with airplane mode on, adding a title succeeds, shows placeholder initials/color-block art, and appears in the correct unranked/pending state immediately.

3. **Placed confirmation screen** — as a user I see exactly where my title landed and what it's adjacent to, as a designed reward moment, not a passive list refresh. *Acceptance:* 0-item and 1-item cold starts produce coherent copy ("first one in..."), never a broken "#1 of 1, ahead of nothing" string.

4. **Ranked list with genre/decade filter chips** — minimal, one-pass filtering off the existing `year`/genre schema fields. *Acceptance:* no-match state shows a nudge, not a blank screen; filters never block or gate the base ranked list.

5. **TV-addable-but-ranking-disabled boundary** — TV titles can be added/logged for free but show a visibly disabled ranking action with a real "Unlock with FaveOff+" CTA. *Acceptance:* adding a TV title never blocks or alters movie ranking state; the disabled row is one clean, non-modal explainer, not a forced interruption.

6. **25-item free movie ranking cap** — enforced with its own non-blocking capped state. *Acceptance:* the cap state appears exactly at the 25th ranked movie; further title adds still succeed; only ranking beyond 25 is blocked.

7. **Taste Card generation + ShareLink** — `ImageRenderer` → branded poster-grid card → native share sheet. *Acceptance:* card is legible at iMessage-thumbnail size with a persistent brand mark; rendered as a static image so later title deletion can never corrupt an already-shared card.

8. **Real sandboxed StoreKit 2 purchase flow** — local `.storekit` config, both subscription products, real buy/pending/failed states, `Transaction.currentEntitlements` reconciliation on launch, visible Restore Purchases. *Acceptance:* a forced network failure or slow checkout stays fully contained to the purchase sheet and leaves the ranking loop completely unaffected — a real test case, not an assumption.

9. **Crash/interruption-safe persistence (including delete-safety)** — insertion state (`rankingState`, persisted `lo`/`hi`/`lastComparedIndex`/`skipBudget`) is always derived from SwiftData truth, never assumed in-memory. This must-have's acceptance criteria explicitly cover deletion, since it's the same failure category (the thing an in-flight insertion referenced is gone): deleting a title mid-active-comparison cancels that insertion cleanly, returns to the list with explicit "title removed, comparison canceled" copy, no crash, no dangling comparison references. *Acceptance:* kill the app mid-insertion, relaunch, and it resumes from persisted bounds exactly; delete a title while it's on-screen in a live comparison and the app recovers cleanly instead of crashing.

10. **Accessibility as a build gate on the Compare and Placed screens specifically** — measured contrast ≥4.5:1 body-weight / ≥3:1 large-bold text across the full Dynamic Type range, VoiceOver labels on every actionable element, 44pt touch targets. *Acceptance:* a Compare or Placed screen that hasn't been contrast-checked isn't considered a finished must-have — it's an incomplete one. This is folded directly into must-haves #2 and #3's definition of done, not a freestanding polish pass.

**Additional testable acceptance framing that applies across the whole must-have list:** count the taps between "user just added their 3rd movie" and "user is looking at their ranked list of 3" — if the TV-lock explainer, cap warning, or paywall CTA appears anywhere in that path unprompted (without the user tapping something that leads there), that's a failure. Policy/boundary screens are allowed to exist; they're not allowed to intrude on a path nobody asked to be shown.

**Should-have:** metadata enrichment via an optional protocol-backed provider (offline stub remains the default, never load-bearing); per-title "confidence" indicator showing how many real comparisons it's actually been through; delete-as-a-discoverable-UI-affordance (where the delete action actually lives — swipe, detail screen, etc. — as opposed to delete-safety itself, which is a Must); non-blocking "similar title already exists" duplicate nudge; light micro-haptics on comparison taps.

**Could-have:** brand-forward visual/motion polish (film-reel transitions) beyond the restrained baseline; filter-selection persistence across sessions; queue-depth indicator for rapid multi-add (explicitly deferred, separable from the crash-safety persistence which is already a Must); CSV/history import.

**Won't-build:** WidgetKit, tutorial/onboarding screens, manual drag-to-reorder, social graph/feed, periodic recap cards, saved/named sub-rank profiles, music/album ranking track, live metadata/poster API as a load-bearing dependency, push notifications, custom Photos-save button, data export/backup beyond the Taste Card image, classic Elo, full Ford-Johnson merge-insertion, full round-robin n² comparisons, forced re-comparisons.

---

CONSENSUS: YES

# faveoff — Design Discussion

_Generated by the autonomous multi-agent orchestrator on 2026-07-08 12:33:26._

## Original Prompt

```
PORTFOLIO_CHILD_PROJECT: true
Parent portfolio: multi-app-exp7
Selected app slug: faveoff

Build this app as a working local-first SwiftUI MVP.

## Selected App Spec

# FaveOff

Build mode: **MVP build**.
Build priority: **4**.

## App Name

FaveOff

## Category

Entertainment / Taste Ranking

## One-Sentence Promise

Rank the movies and shows you've watched head-to-head, and FaveOff builds your real, personal top 10 — then turns it into a shareable taste card.

## Target User

Anyone who watches movies/shows and has opinions but finds star ratings meaningless and social-network movie apps like overhead — mass-market watchers, not cinephile completionists.

## Painful Moment Solved

Rating a movie 1-5 stars and realizing that number means nothing next to the 40 other movies you've also rated 4 stars — you genuinely don't know your own top 10.

## Why It Is Different

Instead of star ratings, shows two watched titles and asks which was better, building an Elo-style ranked list from a handful of quick head-to-head picks — far more decisive than star ratings, ending in a poster-grid 'Taste Card' built for sharing, with zero social graph or account required to get value.

## Why Users Would Pay

Free tier ranks movies only with a capped list length; FaveOff+ unlocks TV shows and music/albums as separate ranking tracks, unlimited list length, genre-filtered sub-rankings, and periodic 'Wrapped'-style recap cards.

## Subscription Model

Freemium, $3.99/mo or $24.99/yr.

## Local-First MVP Scope

SwiftData store for titles, comparisons, and computed rankings; ranking algorithm runs fully on-device; title/poster metadata lookup is the one network dependency, isolated behind a protocol.

## Future Backend Roadmap

Repository boundary allows CloudKit sync across devices and, later, an optional social taste-comparison layer without touching the core ranking engine.

## AR / Local ML / Local LLM Fit

None load-bearing; the hard technical capability is a proper Elo/merge-sort-style pairwise ranking engine, not ML.

## Design Direction

Cinematic dark-mode-first system — near-black backgrounds, marquee-red accent, bold condensed poster-style typography, poster artwork as the dominant visual element, film-reel-inspired transitions.

## App Store Positioning

Star ratings lie to you. FaveOff makes you pick, one match-up at a time, until your real top 10 falls out.

## Invalidation Criteria

If users abandon mid-ranking after only a handful of comparisons and never reach a shareable top-10, the comparison flow is too tedious and needs a faster convergence algorithm or smaller default list size.

## v1 Build Scope

Add titles (movies+TV), head-to-head ranking engine, ranked list view, Taste Card generation + ShareLink, FaveOff+ paywall with StoreKit.

## Core Workflows

- add a watched title
- head-to-head comparison rounds
- browse computed ranked list
- generate & share Taste Card (poster grid of top 10)
- filter rankings by genre/decade

## iOS-Native Features

- ShareLink for Taste Card
- ImageRenderer for poster-grid card generation
- WidgetKit small widget showing current #1

## Key Risks

- The one network touchpoint (title/poster lookup) must degrade gracefully offline (initials/color-block placeholder), never blocking the core ranking loop
- Comparison fatigue on large libraries — needs a smart pairing algorithm scaling ~n log n, not full round-robin n²

## Claude Design Handoff Prompt

Design a cinematic entertainment-ranking app called FaveOff: near-black dark-mode-first backgrounds, marquee-red accent, bold condensed poster-style typography, movie/show poster art as the dominant visual content, film-reel-inspired swipe/transition motion for head-to-head comparison screens, feels like a red-carpet awards show, not a spreadsheet.

This is a multi-app PORTFOLIO request: multiple apps in one program. Each selected app becomes its own separate project — one folder per app, never bundled together.

FOURTH PRIORITY — MONETIZATION:

General Requirements:
```
