# faveoff — Design Handoff

_Generated by the autonomous multi-agent orchestrator on 2026-07-08 12:37:11._

## Original Prompt

```
PORTFOLIO_CHILD_PROJECT: true
Parent portfolio: multi-app-exp7
Selected app slug: faveoff

Build this app as a working local-first SwiftUI MVP.

## Selected App Spec

# FaveOff

Build mode: **MVP build**.
Build priority: **4**.

## App Name

FaveOff

## Category

Entertainment / Taste Ranking

## One-Sentence Promise

Rank the movies and shows you've watched head-to-head, and FaveOff builds your real, personal top 10 — then turns it into a shareable taste card.

## Target User

Anyone who watches movies/shows and has opinions but finds star ratings meaningless and social-network movie apps like overhead — mass-market watchers, not cinephile completionists.

## Painful Moment Solved

Rating a movie 1-5 stars and realizing that number means nothing next to the 40 other movies you've also rated 4 stars — you genuinely don't know your own top 10.

## Why It Is Different

Instead of star ratings, shows two watched titles and asks which was better, building an Elo-style ranked list from a handful of quick head-to-head picks — far more decisive than star ratings, ending in a poster-grid 'Taste Card' built for sharing, with zero social graph or account required to get value.

## Why Users Would Pay

Free tier ranks movies only with a capped list length; FaveOff+ unlocks TV shows and music/albums as separate ranking tracks, unlimited list length, genre-filtered sub-rankings, and periodic 'Wrapped'-style recap cards.

## Subscription Model

Freemium, $3.99/mo or $24.99/yr.

## Local-First MVP Scope

SwiftData store for titles, comparisons, and computed rankings; ranking algorithm runs fully on-device; title/poster metadata lookup is the one network dependency, isolated behind a protocol.

## Future Backend Roadmap

Repository boundary allows CloudKit sync across devices and, later, an optional social taste-comparison layer without touching the core ranking engine.

## AR / Local ML / Local LLM Fit

None load-bearing; the hard technical capability is a proper Elo/merge-sort-style pairwise ranking engine, not ML.

## Design Direction

Cinematic dark-mode-first system — near-black backgrounds, marquee-red accent, bold condensed poster-style typography, poster artwork as the dominant visual element, film-reel-inspired transitions.

## App Store Positioning

Star ratings lie to you. FaveOff makes you pick, one match-up at a time, until your real top 10 falls out.

## Invalidation Criteria

If users abandon mid-ranking after only a handful of comparisons and never reach a shareable top-10, the comparison flow is too tedious and needs a faster convergence algorithm or smaller default list size.

## v1 Build Scope

Add titles (movies+TV), head-to-head ranking engine, ranked list view, Taste Card generation + ShareLink, FaveOff+ paywall with StoreKit.

## Core Workflows

- add a watched title
- head-to-head comparison rounds
- browse computed ranked list
- generate & share Taste Card (poster grid of top 10)
- filter rankings by genre/decade

## iOS-Native Features

- ShareLink for Taste Card
- ImageRenderer for poster-grid card generation
- WidgetKit small widget showing current #1

## Key Risks

- The one network touchpoint (title/poster lookup) must degrade gracefully offline (initials/color-block placeholder), never blocking the core ranking loop
- Comparison fatigue on large libraries — needs a smart pairing algorithm scaling ~n log n, not full round-robin n²

## Claude Design Handoff Prompt

Design a cinematic entertainment-ranking app called FaveOff: near-black dark-mode-first backgrounds, marquee-red accent, bold condensed poster-style typography, movie/show poster art as the dominant visual content, film-reel-inspired swipe/transition motion for head-to-head comparison screens, feels like a red-carpet awards show, not a spreadsheet.

## Parent Portfolio Prompt

Build 5 completely separate, production-ready iOS apps at the same time.

This is a multi-app PORTFOLIO request: multiple apps in one program. Each selected app becomes its own separate project — one folder per app, never bundled together.

Pick any 5 categories/concepts — no fixed assignment. Choose the 5 strongest MASS-MARKET ideas.

TOP PRIORITY — VIRAL, MASS-MARKET APPEAL (hard requirement):
- Every app must target a BROAD, mainstream audience. NO niche-community apps (no hobbyist tools, no professional verticals, no special-interest utilities). If your parents, your barista, and a college student wouldn't all instantly get it, it fails.
- Each app must have a built-in viral loop: something people naturally share, screenshot, send to friends, or compete over (shareable results/cards, streaks, challenges, comparisons, "send this to a friend" moments).
- The concept must be explainable in one sentence and demo-able in 10 seconds.
- Think the scale of habit trackers, photo tools, social utilities, everyday life tools, casual self-improvement, money, food, sleep, relationships, entertainment — things with tens of millions of potential users.

SECOND PRIORITY — DESIGN & UI/UX:
- World-class, distinctive visual identity per app: deliberate color system, type scale, spacing, iconography, motion language — not stock SwiftUI defaults.
- "Apple Design Award" caliber polish. Every screen considered: empty, loading, error states, transitions, haptics, spring animations.
- Dark mode, Dynamic Type, accessibility (VoiceOver, 44pt targets, WCAG AA contrast).
- Each of the 5 apps must look clearly DIFFERENT from the other four — five distinct design directions.
- Documented design system per app (tokens, components, states); built UI must match it.

THIRD PRIORITY — COMPLEXITY & DEPTH:
- Genuinely substantial apps — interconnected features, real domain data model, non-trivial logic, offline persistence with sync-later architecture, robust settings, import/export, and at least one hard technical capability done well.
- Depth that rewards daily use — but never at the expense of instant mass-market clarity.

FOURTH PRIORITY — MONETIZATION:
- Clear, sustainable revenue path per app (subscription tiers, IAP, premium) that feels native to the product, never punitive. Free tier hooks users; paid tier is compelling.

General Requirements:
1. Each app goes from 0 to production-ready.
2. Each app must be unique, useful, and commercially viable, better than competitors in a meaningful way.
3. Local-first, architected so cloud support can be added later without rewriting.

UNIQUENESS (hard rule, non-negotiable):
- No app in this batch may be similar to another app in this batch.
- No app may be similar to anything already built in this workspace: Waylay, TrueScale, Provenance, Scope-Guard, Practice-Loop, Proof, ReturnWise, VerveCoach, CueKeeper, brinekeeper, chalkline, quietpilot, cistern, thicket, undersong, undertow, heddle, marginalia, skyglass, and the ~50 apps of prior batches (aarti-path, afterglow, backtimer, chapterly, cleanline, clearpath-access, crockwatch, crosswire, crux-cycle, curio, dayframe, dharma-trail, docket, flinch, heartline, kitbag, night-sky-logbook, pattern-pulse, pillcheck, referral-loop, risk-compass, rollcall, runway, scenario-ledger, shelf, sincerely, tabzero, talkthrough, tessella, throughline, trestle, triptally, umbra, wordsmith-atlas, and others).
- Produce differentiated concepts even if a category overlaps.

Bonus points (optional, only when integral): unique use of LLMs, AR, or ML.

Build rules:
- Run the five app efforts in parallel; keep phases separate per app.
- TIME MATTERS on this batch: reach consensus quickly in discussion phases (end phases early on agreement), but never skip the build, and never mark an app done unless a real, compiling Xcode project with full source exists in app_build. An app without a buildable Xcode project is a FAILURE.
- Save all phase discussions; combine each app's transcript into a .txt at the end.
- Recover cleanly from stalls; report only important milestones.

Output:
- One folder per app in the output directory — never one folder containing all apps.
Output directory:
- /Users/pchordia/Documents/iOS-App-Factory

Now generate at least 10 candidate ideas, judge them primarily on mass-market viral potential, choose the best 5, and build all 5 to production quality with exceptional UI/UX.
```

## Phase Purpose

Produce a complete design handoff: screen-by-screen specs, interaction states, motion notes, design tokens, accessibility expectations, and a professional Claude Design prompt or import plan if the run is configured to pause for external design.

## Transcript


### Round 1


**Codex (Design Lead · User Advocate) — Round 1**

I would build FaveOff around one root screen and a strict ritual: add → compare → placed → continue. Everything else is support for that loop and never an interruption.

The core feeling target is a single-tap, no-think decision ritual with film-club energy: two cards, one tap, immediate placement, then a short dopamine beat. If the user can’t tell what to do on Compare in 1 second, the design fails. Biggest objection to this direction is the absence of onboarding; I am accepting that risk because it preserves speed, but every Compare affordance must be unmistakable so it never feels like a dead form.

Library
Root is a single `NavigationStack` (no tabs, no alternate ranked home). Top line is segmented control for Movies | TV to make track boundaries obvious. Under it, filter chips for Genre and Decade appear only when filtering has value (minimum meaningful item count, e.g., 5+ ranked/eligible titles). Main content is the ranked list; each row is poster-first, compact text secondary, 44pt+ tap row targets, and TV rows show lock treatment instead of rank if not entitled. Toolbar actions: Add Title and Share Card. A compact top banner is only shown on relaunch if there is a persisted in-flight insertion to resume. A capped-state banner is inline at the bottom of the list when movie ranking is blocked at the 25-item free limit. Empty state shows “Add your first movie” as the primary CTA and a short confidence-safe sentence about “just add one title to begin.” Loading state can be a skeleton of top rows with shimmer placeholders. Error state is explicit inline card with retry and a one-line actionable hint.
Interaction states: empty, list, listWithFilters, emptyFiltered, resumeBanner, movieCapReachedBanner, loading, error.
Accessibility in Library: dynamic text friendly layout, row labels include index, title, year, media type; VoiceOver should read “1, movie title, 2021, unlocked,” or “locked by taste track.”
Navigation: from row tap, optional detail view; from Share Card opens Taste Card sheet; from add/cap/TV lock opens modal flows.

Add Title
Medium detent sheet with minimal fields: title (autofocus), media type segmented control (Movie/TV), optional year, optional genre. Save must succeed fully offline and return immediately to local state. Two call-to-action buttons: Cancel and Add. Optional metadata enrichment is async, optional, and never blocks.
States: idle, saving, success (returns to Library), failed (network or validation).
Error behavior: show retry inline, do not lose typed text.
Accessibility: field labels explicit, required/optional indicator for year/genre, large tap targets.
Content hierarchy: title first, then type, then optional metadata; action row pinned bottom to avoid drifting focus.

Compare
Full-screen cover with no visible parent chrome. Two large poster cards side by side (or identical-weight initials/color blocks if poster missing), short title labels beneath each, divider with VS motif, one secondary Skip control below. There are no progress bars, checklists, counters, or confidence meters while deciding; trust is reported after the placement event, not during the decision.
States: pairReady, choicePending, skipPending, terminatedBySkips (fallback to nearest edge placement), interrupted (opposing title missing), loading if assets hydrate, error only if internal fetch/cache failure for persisted data.
Actions: select left, select right, Skip. Tie is not a separate action and is merged into Skip.
Motion: Compare/Placed transition uses a fast film-reel directional wipe; Reduce Motion => crossfade.
Accessibility: each poster action label must be explicit (“Choose [Title A] over [Title B]” / “Choose [Title B] over [Title A]”), Skip read as “Skip this comparison,” 44pt minimum hit targets at the smallest layouts, contrast checks met (≥4.5:1 body, ≥3:1 large/bold) before consider done.
If user deletes the item being compared, immediately cancel back to Library with recoverable toast (“title removed, comparison canceled”) and no crash.

Placed
Immediate full-screen confirmation beat, one purpose: reward and communicate placement honestly. Show rank number in display typography and neighbor context:
- exact placement: “#N, ahead of X, behind Y”
- 0/1 item insertion: “First one in — add another to build your ranking”
- skip fallback with no reliable comparisons: “Added near the bottom for now — compare it later to refine”
Call to action: Keep going (single).
State variants: exactPlacement, fallbackPlacement, coldStartPlacement, loadingPoster, errorRender (very rare; render placeholders and allow retry).
Accessibility: VO announcement includes title, rank, and two neighbors in one concise sentence; avoid long paragraphs.

Taste Card
Large-detent sheet from Library toolbar. Generated using `ImageRenderer` preview at first then shareable final image.
Card layout is 3x? dynamic poster grid with Top 10, small brand mark + app name at top/bottom, date stamp optional, muted backdrop to keep it thumbnail-legible. Share via native `ShareLink`.
States: loading/preparing image, rendered-ready, share-error, retry.
Accessibility and contrast apply to preview labels and button labels; avoid tiny text that disappears when exported.

Paywall
Single reusable sheet reused for cap banner, TV lock row, and upgrade action.
States: idle, pricingLoaded, purchaseInProgress, purchasePending, purchaseFailed, restored, purchased.
Failure paths must remain fully contained inside sheet; ranking and add/compare loops continue independently.
Restore action always visible.
No full-screen modal chains from core loop; ranking remains usable while user exits.

Library row and card components
PosterTile (rounded rectangle image/placeholder initials block, title subline, year, rank badge optional)
TrackChip (genre/decade filter chips, compact, multi-select reset)
RankBadge (condensed number + high-contrast ring)
LockedTVRow (lock + unlock copy + CTA)
ResumeBanner (title + Continue button)
CapBanner (limit reached + upgrade)
Contrast-safe body tokens and action chips should be reusable so the same hierarchy appears in Browse/Compare/Placed language.

Design system
Background: near-black warm charcoal (`#111217`), secondary surface `#1A1A1F`, elevated surface `#202126`.
Accent: marquee red (`#D31A3B`) for primary actions only.
Text: system font for body/labels (`Body`, `Callout`, `Headline`) for accessibility integrity; condensed display style only for short strings (rank numbers, “PLACED”, section labels).
Border/ring: muted warm gray outlines for non-action surfaces (`#2C2C33`), only red borders for primary focus.
Radius: 14pt cards, 10pt chips, 12pt buttons.
Spacing: 4pt rhythm grid: 4/8/12/16/20/24.
Animation: fast and restrained; compare/placed wipe with subtle sprocket-like horizontal texture; spring on row insertion; haptics on successful tap and keep-going action.
Motion guard: if reduce motion on, convert wipe to quick fade, disable non-essential parallax.

Critical interaction obligations that must be preserved
One-step fast loop from add to placed.
No surprise modal interruptions on free flow path (for fresh movie adds under cap).
TV titles can be added but are ranking-locked without upgrade.
25-title movie free cap blocks ranking only, not adding.
Skip fallback is deterministic and preserves ordering consistency.
Deleted/removed title while comparing cancels safely and recoverably.
Every screen with possible failure includes explicit error state, not silent fail.

Accessibility constraints for this pass
WCAG AA checks across Dynamic Type for Compare and Placed before calling them complete.
Body contrast ≥4.5:1, large/bold ≥3:1.
VoiceOver for each actionable control.
No color-only status indication.
44×44 minimum targets on all controls and cards.

Component inventory
`NavigationShell`, `LibraryList`, `RankedRow`, `PosterTile`, `TrackSegmentedControl`, `FilterChip`, `Banner`, `AddTitleSheet`, `CompareView`, `PlacedView`, `TasteCardSheet`, `CardShareButton`, `PaywallSheet`, `DeleteAction` (swipe + overflow menu fallback), `EmptyStateView`, `ErrorStateView`, `LoadingStateView`.
State inventory per requirement
Each reachable screen has empty/loading/success/error variants where applicable, and deterministic transition behavior so crash recovery is safe.

Claude Design prompt/upload instruction (if you want external design pass)
Use this prompt directly for an external design handoff:
“Design an iOS app called FaveOff with a single-screen root Library flow and cinematic red-carpet motif. Style: near-black dark warm palette, high-contrast red accent, poster-first composition, condensed rank typography for short text only, bold but accessible controls, no onboarding. Build a ritualized compare flow using two large movie/show cards with explicit tappable affordances, secondary Skip action, and instant placement screen that states ‘#N, ahead of..., behind...’ with clear edge-case copy for first-item and skip-fallback paths. Keep policy screens (cap/upgrade/TV lock) non-blocking and contextual. Include full empty/loading/error states, reduced-motion alternatives, and VoiceOver-friendly labels. Output as detailed screen-by-screen SwiftUI-ready spec with component spacing, states, transitions, and accessibility checks.”

**Claude (Frontend Engineer · Systems Thinker) — Round 1**

The thing I want to nail down this round that hasn't actually been specified yet, despite five phases of solid product decisions, is the navigation architecture underneath the screen list — because the locked flow (Add chains into Compare chains into Placed, plus a resume banner that has to jump into that same Compare state from cold launch) is exactly the shape that produces SwiftUI's ugliest class of bug if we don't pin it down now: competing presentation state. If Compare is one `.fullScreenCover(isPresented:)` and Placed is a second, separate `.fullScreenCover(isPresented:)`, dismissing one to present the other causes a visible flash back to Library in between — which directly undercuts the "total focus, red carpet" feeling both of the last two phases fought hard to establish. My strong recommendation: Compare and Placed are not two covers, they're one `.fullScreenCover(item:)` bound to a single `ActiveInsertionFlow` value (holding the persisted `lo/hi/lastComparedIndex/skipBudget` plus an internal `.comparing`/`.placed(result)` sub-state), and the transition between comparing and placed happens as an internal view swap inside that one cover, not a dismiss-then-present. The resume banner at the top of Library, when tapped, constructs that exact same `ActiveInsertionFlow` value from persisted state and sets it — so there's exactly one code path into Compare, whether you just tapped Add or you're recovering from a crash three days later. I'd also put Add Title, Taste Card, and Paywall behind their own single `.sheet(item:)` at the Library root, each driven by an enum case, so there is never a state where two presentations could theoretically be true at once — that invariant is worth writing into the handoff explicitly because it's the kind of thing that's easy to violate two screens later when someone adds a "quick share" button from inside Placed without realizing it collides with the cover already on screen.

Second systems-level risk worth flagging now: the Taste Card is generated with `ImageRenderer` off the ranked list's top ten, but if any of that art is an `AsyncImage` still resolving (or a placeholder still animating in), the rendered card can silently capture blank or half-loaded squares — `ImageRenderer` snapshots exactly what's laid out at that instant, it doesn't wait for async image loads the way the live view does. Since our default happy path is offline/no-network placeholder art (deterministic initials + color block, generated synchronously from the title string, no network round trip), this is mostly a non-issue for the default provider — but it's a real trap the moment the optional metadata-enrichment provider is wired in later, and it's a trap even now if we're not careful about resolving all ten poster views into fully-loaded `UIImage`/`Image` values before triggering the render. I want this written into the handoff as a rule: Taste Card sheet has its own brief `resolvingArt` loading state before the live preview appears, and the render only fires once every one of the up-to-ten source images is confirmed loaded, never on a best-effort snapshot.

On the actual visual system: background is a warm near-black, not true black — `#15100F` for the base canvas, one step up `#1E1918` for cards/rows, `#272020` for sheet/elevated surfaces, with a `#33292A` hairline used for card borders instead of drop shadows — shadows barely read against a near-black field and cost more to render for zero visual gain, a bordered-card model is both more idiomatic for this palette and cheaper. Marquee red is a single accent, `#E22C3C`, used only for fills (primary buttons, the rank-reveal number treatment on Placed, active segmented-control state) and for icon/glyph accents — never as a body-text color, because red-on-charcoal body text can't reliably clear 4.5:1 across the Dynamic Type range no matter how we tune it, and locked policy already says body text must hit that gate. Actual text colors: primary text near-white `#F6F1ED` on the charcoal base (contrast well past 4.5:1 at any size), secondary/metadata text a warm muted gray `#AFA7A2` (also independently checked to clear 4.5:1 for body weight, not just "looks fine"), and red is only ever allowed on large/bold display strings (the rank number itself, which is both large and bold by construction) where the 3:1 threshold applies. For typography, I want us to explicitly avoid bundling a custom condensed font file — iOS's system font already exposes a condensed width trait (`Font.system(size:weight:design:).width(.condensed)`, available iOS 16+), which scales correctly with Dynamic Type and carries zero risk of a missing/unembedded font asset breaking a headless build. Condensed-bold is reserved for exactly the short strings locked earlier — rank numbers, the Placed hero title, section headers, chip labels — every other string (body copy, filter labels, VoiceOver-read text, anything that can wrap) uses the standard system width at full Dynamic Type range.

Spacing runs an 8pt base grid (4/8/12/16/24/32/48), card corner radius 16pt, buttons 14pt, filter chips fully rounded. Motion: a shared 0.25s ease-in-out for ordinary state changes (list updates, banner appearance), and a distinct ~0.35s "reel wipe" custom `AnyTransition` for the one moment that's allowed to feel cinematic — the swap from Compare into Placed inside that single full-screen cover — built as a horizontal masked wipe with a faint sprocket-hole edge detail, collapsing to a flat 0.2s opacity crossfade whenever `accessibilityReduceMotion` is on, no exceptions, no partial-motion compromise.

Component inventory a builder can actually implement against: `PosterCard` (poster image or deterministic initials/color-block fallback, identical size/weight either way, used in Library rows, Compare, and the Taste Card grid — one component, three contexts, so the "no visual tell for missing art" rule from the last phase is enforced by construction rather than by convention); `RankBadge` (condensed-bold number, red fill, used in list rows and the Placed hero); `CompareDivider` ("VS" mark between the two `PosterCard`s); `SkipButton` (visually secondary, explicit "Skip this comparison" accessibility label); `FilterChip` (pill, selected/unselected state, appears only past the locked title-count threshold); `LockedRow` (dimmed `PosterCard` + lock glyph + "Unlock with FaveOff+" label, used for TV rows); `InlineBanner` (shared component styled two ways — the cap-reached banner and the crash-recovery resume banner — same visual language, different copy/action, so we're not inventing two banner styles); `PrimaryButton`/`SecondaryButton` (fill vs. outline, both ≥44pt tall); `PaywallProductCard` (price, billing period, buy-state affordance). Reusing `PosterCard` and `InlineBanner` across contexts instead of building near-duplicate one-off views is the kind of detail that keeps this from sprawling into twice the SwiftUI surface area it needs.

State/interaction notes worth locking now beyond what's already in the screen inventory: every list-driving view reads through the repository protocol via `@Observable` view models, never touches `SwiftData` `@Query` directly in a view that also contains navigation logic, consistent with the architecture boundary locked back in the product brief — this matters concretely here because the `ActiveInsertionFlow` state needs to be hydratable from persisted SwiftData truth on cold launch before the first frame renders the resume banner, and that's a view-model responsibility, not a view one. Compare's tap targets are the full `PosterCard` bounds, not a small button overlay on the poster — both cards need `.accessibilityElement(children: .combine)` with the locked "Select [Title A] over [Title B]" label so VoiceOver users get one swipeable choice per card, not a pile of sub-element noise.

Biggest objection to my own direction: collapsing Compare/Placed into a single stateful cover is more SwiftUI plumbing up front than two independent boolean-driven covers would be, and if whoever implements it doesn't respect the "one enum, one source of truth" discipline, it's just as easy to reintroduce the exact bug I'm trying to prevent by nesting ad-hoc state inside that cover's content view. The thing that has to survive into the final handoff isn't just "use one cover" as an instruction — it's the specific rule that ActiveInsertionFlow is owned once, at the Library root, and nothing downstream constructs a competing presentation.

On external design: I don't think this app needs a Claude Design pass for its UI chrome — every screen, token, and component here is specific enough for a SwiftUI builder to implement directly, and introducing an external design round-trip for token-driven system-font layouts would add a sync step without adding craft. The one asset that genuinely benefits from a dedicated design pass is the app icon, since that's a single hand-crafted image or Design Sync in a way that a code-driven token system doesn't cover — I'd scope a Claude Design prompt narrowly to that: "Design an app icon for FaveOff, a cinematic movie/TV ranking app — near-black warm charcoal base (#15100F), marquee red accent (#E22C3C), bold condensed numeral or ticket/film-reel motif, award-show/red-carpet feeling, must read clearly at 60x60pt down to the smallest home-screen size, no text." Everything else — posters, placeholder art, cards, banners — is generated or laid out in code and doesn't need an external design round trip.

**Coordinator (Claude) — decision after round 1**

CONSENSUS: NO

Here's where round 1 actually lands. Both sides agree completely on the big structural stuff carried over from earlier phases — single Library root, no tabs, the Compare/Placed ritual with no progress meter, tie merged into skip, TV-locked rows, non-blocking cap/paywall banners, the resume banner for crash recovery, and the accessibility gates (4.5:1 body / 3:1 large-bold, 44pt targets, VoiceOver labels describing the actual choice). Codex delivered the full screen-by-screen spec with states, component inventory, and design tokens; Claude (in the systems-engineer chair this round) went deeper on the plumbing underneath it — flagging a real risk that Compare and Placed as two separate `.fullScreenCover(isPresented:)` booleans would cause a visible flash back to Library between them, and proposing they be one `.fullScreenCover(item:)` driven by a single `ActiveInsertionFlow` enum instead, with the resume banner constructing that same value. Claude also caught a genuine `ImageRenderer` trap: it snapshots synchronously, so any still-loading `AsyncImage` art would silently render blank in the Taste Card, and proposed a `resolvingArt` loading state before the card preview appears. Neither of these contradicts Codex's spec — they're implementation-level detail Codex's draft left open — but Codex hasn't had a chance to weigh in on them yet.

There are two real disagreements still sitting on the table, not just phrasing gaps:

1. **Two different color token systems.** Codex specified background `#111217`/`#1A1A1F`/`#202126`, accent `#D31A3B`. Claude independently specified background `#15100F`/`#1E1918`/`#272020` with a `#33292A` hairline border, accent `#E22C3C`, plus explicit measured text colors (`#F6F1ED` primary, `#AFA7A2` secondary). These are two different palettes for the same "near-black warm charcoal, marquee red" direction — the handoff can't ship with both.

2. **Whether to use an external Claude Design pass at all, and for what.** Codex wrote a full Claude Design prompt meant to hand off the *entire* screen-by-screen UI to an external design pass. Claude argued the opposite — that the token/component system here is already specific enough for a SwiftUI builder to implement directly, external design would just add a sync step, and the *only* thing worth a dedicated Claude Design prompt is the app icon, with its own narrowly-scoped prompt. That's a direct disagreement on scope, not just style.

Next round needs Codex and Claude to pick one color palette (or reconcile them into one token set) and agree on whether Claude Design gets used for the full UI, just the icon, or not at all.

### Round 2


**Codex (Design Lead · User Advocate) — Round 2**

Codex, I’m with your overall direction that this should stay brutal-simple for v1, but I’m not signing off on a full external Claude design pass for the whole UI right now. You’re already giving enough precision for a builder to implement directly, and pausing for a full design round-trip would actually re-introduce ambiguity at the exact edges that matter: `ActiveInsertionFlow` state, compare interruption recovery, and asynchronous art rendering in `ImageRenderer`. The single external pass I *do* want is icon-only because that’s one artifact that’s hard to infer from SwiftUI tokens.

Claude, your implementation warning is exactly right and should be treated as a hard rule in this handoff: compare and placed cannot be two independent covers. They must be one full-screen cover driven by one flow model, so there is never a dismiss-flash between decision and placement. I’m carrying that through and baking it into navigation/state, because this app’s feel depends on that continuous ritual.

Palette: I’m choosing the warm-charcoal + accent direction from your proposal, but I’m tightening it to hard tokens with predictable contrast math (so we can actually verify AA). Base family: near-black charcoal, no pure blacks, no red body text.
- backgroundCanvas = `#15100F`
- backgroundSurface = `#1E1918`
- backgroundElevated = `#272020`
- borderLine = `#33292A`
- accentRed = `#E22C3C` (action-only and large short strings)
- textPrimary = `#F6F1ED`
- textSecondary = `#AFA7A2`
- textDisabled = `#6F6660`
Use `Font.system` widths where possible, with condensed width only for short labels (rank number, “PLACED”, rank headers, chip labels). No condensed custom font file; dynamic scaling behavior has to stay intact.

Screen inventory stays exactly six roots plus the compare flow detail and no-tab structure: Library, Add Title, Compare, Placed, Taste Card, Paywall. Library is the one and only root `NavigationStack` entrypoint (no tabs, no second browse screen). The compare/placed ritual is a dedicated full-screen flow on top. I’m keeping this strict because every extra surface risks “what am I supposed to do now?” and this app should feel like one button at a time.

On Library, I want it to be immediately understandable for a distracted user: segmented Movies | TV at the top to teach the track boundary, optional filters only when useful, then the ranked list itself. For first-run users, the empty-state CTA is “Add your first movie.” with no preface text-heavy onboarding. If there are enough entries, show genre and decade chips, otherwise suppress chips so the screen stays light and not “menu soup.” TV rows are always visible but rank lock state is visual and explicit (dimmed poster, lock glyph, “Unlock with FaveOff+”); they never block add or movie ranking.

The compare flow is one-screen ritual by design: two full poster cards, same visual weight whether art exists or placeholders are used, divider with VS motif, and one secondary action labeled “Skip.” There is no progress bar, no count of remaining comparisons, no confidence meter. “Tie” is not a separate action; it is Skip. This keeps the decision binary and preserves the sort invariant while matching the trust model from earlier phases. If either compared title disappears (including deletion mid-flow), cancel gracefully to Library with explicit toast text “Title removed, comparison canceled.” No crash, no frozen screen.

Placed is the reward beat and not another utility screen. If exact placement, it always says `#N, ahead of X, behind Y`. If cold-start edge cases, it uses the dedicated non-broken text and never says “ahead of nothing” in the wrong way. If skip-terminated with no real comparisons, it uses explicit honesty copy (“Added near the bottom for now — compare later to refine.”), which is part of trust management for this app. One action on Placed: “Keep going.”

Add Title is a medium-detent quick sheet: title first, type selector second, year + genre optional and visually secondary. Save should be instant and local first, then route either to compare or placed depending on rank state (0/1-item or no-op path). If saving fails (validation/storage glitch), preserve typed content, don’t drop input, show inline retry.

Taste Card is a large-detent sheet from Library toolbar icon, with a real loading state before preview. There is a deliberate rule from Claude’s systems note that I’m adopting: do not snapshot before art is resolved in-memory, so taste card preview/render should not fire on partially loaded posters. Since offline placeholder art is default, this is easy in MVP, but still enforce loading readiness state so the feature doesn’t regress when optional enrichment is added later.

Paywall is one shared sheet used from all entry points (TV lock, cap banner, toolbar upgrade). It handles loading/pending/failed/purchased/restore and remains dismissible; ranking should never be blocked while a failed purchase is handled.

Now the concrete state map, because this is what the build should target:
- Library: `loading`, `emptyNoItems`, `list`, `listWithFilters`, `emptyFiltered`, `loadingFailure`, plus two banners as overlays: `resumeInsertion` (only if persisted in-flight insertion found on relaunch) and `capReached` (inline bottom).
- Add Title: `idle`, `saving`, `saved`, `failed`.
- Active insertion flow (Compare/Placed single cover): `comparing`, `processingChoice`, `skip`, `terminatedBySkips`, `cancelledByInterruption`, `placed`.
- Placed variants: `exact`, `fallback`, `coldStart`.
- Taste Card: `resolvingArt`, `ready`, `renderFailed`, `sharing`.
- Paywall: `available`, `loadingProducts`, `purchasePending`, `purchaseFailed`, `restored`, `entitled`, `dismissed`.

Navigation/state implementation notes I’m locking:
- Library owns one enum `activeSheet: SheetKind?` and one `activeFlow: ActiveInsertionFlow?`.
- `Add Title`, `Taste Card`, and `Paywall` are separate `.sheet(item:)` from Library.
- Compare + Placed are one `.fullScreenCover(item:)` on `activeFlow` with internal state transitions, so no flash on compare-to-placed handoff.
- Repository reads go through a view model/repo boundary (no direct `@Query` driving navigation decisions in the same view).

Component set I want to be explicit for builder handoff:
`PosterCard`, `RankBadge`, `LibraryRow`, `LockedTVRow`, `FilterChip`, `TrackSegmentedControl`, `InlineBanner`, `EmptyState`, `ErrorState`, `AddTitleFieldRow`, `ComparePosterChoice`, `SkipButton`, `PlacedReveal`, `TasteCardGrid`, `PaywallProductTile`, `PrimaryButton`, `SecondaryButton`, and a small `SectionSpacer` tokenized by spacing scale.

Motion is minimal and cinematic where it counts: one intentional reel-style wipe for Compare→Placed inside the cover, 0.35s, optional subtle sprocket edge; all else uses a fast standard 0.25s fade/slide. If Reduce Motion, everything in that path becomes 0.2s crossfade only.

Accessibility stays non-negotiable gates, not afterthoughts:
- Compare and Placed are the strict gates with checks before done.
- Contrast: body ≥ 4.5:1, large/bold ≥ 3.0:1 across Dynamic Type sizes.
- 44pt minimum tap targets for poster cards and all controls.
- VoiceOver labels as action language, not object labels only: “Select [A] over [B],”
 “Skip this comparison,” and one concise placement sentence including title/rank/adjacent neighbors.
- Never use color-only status cues; lock states should include glyph/text.

Deletion affordance is discoverable but intentionally secondary in this phase: swipe-to-delete in row plus a detail path fallback for people who miss swipe. It supports trust with no crash and no dangling references via the same persistence + resume-safe flow.

On the open disagreement from last round, I’m resolving it like this: we use one palette exactly, based on the explicit text contrast-safe values above, and we do **not** pause this phase for a full external UI design pass. If you want a concrete “external” artifact, use a tiny icon-only request for app icon only, because that’s where external visual authorship materially helps. Full screen-by-screen UI is already fully specified here, so the builder can ship from this handoff directly.

If you want the full external prompt as requested, this is the exact one:
“Design a premium iOS app home+flow for FaveOff: one root Library screen with a cinematic but readable near-black palette, maroon-red accent, poster-first composition, no onboarding, no progress bars in comparisons. Include Add Title sheet, full-screen compare ritual with two tappable poster choices plus secondary Skip, a full-screen Placed reward screen with exact position copy, taste-card sharing flow, and inline banners for TV lock and cap reached. Keep the TV track visible but unlocked for adding, locked for ranking. Provide motion guidance for a single film-reel wipe transition with Reduce Motion crossfade fallback, and include VoiceOver labels + 44pt targets + WCAG AA contrast behavior across Dynamic Type.”

**Claude (Frontend Engineer · Systems Thinker) — Round 2**

Okay, let's actually close both gaps the coordinator flagged, because looking at them side by side I don't think they're evenly matched — one of them has an obvious answer already sitting in a prior locked phase, and the other is worth holding my ground on but with a real compromise offered.

On the palette: Codex, your background sequence is `#111217` → `#1A1A1F` → `#202126`. Look at that base value for a second — R17 G18 B23. Blue channel is the highest of the three. That's a cool, faintly blue-black, not a warm one. Go back to what design_discussion actually locked, word for word: "Dark warm charcoal base (not true black)." That phrase — "warm charcoal" — wasn't invented this round, it's the exact language two phases of debate landed on specifically to avoid the generic-cinematic-app cool-blue-black look that half the App Store already uses for dark mode. My base, `#15100F`, is R21 G16 B15 — red channel highest, genuinely warm, the kind of near-black you get from underexposed film stock rather than a sci-fi UI. This isn't a coin flip between two equally valid takes, it's one palette that satisfies a constraint we already agreed to and one that quietly drifts off it. So I want to hold the line here: warm charcoal base stays mine (`#15100F`/`#1E1918`/`#272020`), and I'll fold in your structural naming — secondary surface, elevated surface — since that's just a labeling convention and doesn't conflict with the actual color values. On the accent, `#D31A3B` versus my `#E22C3C` are close enough in hue that I don't think it's worth fighting over; yours is very slightly darker and I'm fine defaulting to it if you want, as long as it's re-verified against the warm charcoal base for the large-bold 3:1 threshold before anyone treats it as final — small hue shifts can move contrast ratios more than people expect. I do want to keep my measured text tokens though (`#F6F1ED` primary, `#AFA7A2` secondary) since those were the ones I actually reasoned through against the accessibility gate rather than picked on vibes, and nobody's proposed alternate values for those.

Small stuff while we're merging component lists: I like your `DeleteAction` (swipe + overflow menu fallback) — that's the concrete answer to the "where does delete actually live in the UI" Should from the features phase, and I hadn't specified it, so let's take it as-is. Same with your skeleton/shimmer loading state for Library rows — better than my vague "loading" state name. Let's also just pick one name for the poster component instead of both of us independently inventing one — I'll drop `PosterCard` and use your `PosterTile`, doesn't matter which word wins, only that the final doc has one name a builder can grep for.

On the Claude Design question, I want to push back harder, because I don't think this is a "let's split the difference" situation — I think running your full prompt would actively work against the thing we're both trying to protect. Think about what actually happens if we hand "design an iOS app called FaveOff... output as a detailed screen-by-screen SwiftUI-ready spec" to an external design pass that has no visibility into what we just spent two rounds locking: the single-`ActiveInsertionFlow`-cover invariant, the "cap/TV-lock/paywall must never appear unprompted between adding your 3rd movie and seeing your ranked list of 3" rule, the specific honest-copy variants for cold-start and skip-fallback placement. An external design tool doesn't know any of that — it'll produce beautiful frames optimized for looking good in isolation, and the moment those frames imply a different navigation shape (say, a second confirmation screen, or a progress indicator on Compare because it "reads better" visually) we're stuck reconciling a generated design against a state machine it was never told exists. That's not a hypothetical risk, that's just what happens when you hand a fully significant interaction contract to a tool optimizing for visual composition rather than navigation correctness. We'd end up with two sources of truth and a builder forced to pick which one to trust.

So here's what I'll actually offer as a real compromise, not just "no": if you still want visual validation beyond what token values and component descriptions on a page can give you, let's scope a Claude Design prompt to a small, bounded "key-screen mood check" — three static frames only (Library with a few ranked rows, Compare mid-decision, Placed reward state), generated strictly from the tokens and layout rules already locked in this document, explicitly framed as a visual sanity check, not a replacement spec, and explicitly told not to invent new navigation, new copy, or new states. That gets you a real look at whether the warm-charcoal-plus-marquee-red system actually reads as premium in practice, without letting an external pass become a shadow source of truth that competes with the interaction contract we've spent five phases locking. And I'd still keep the icon prompt as its own separate, narrow ask, since that's a case where a single hand-crafted image genuinely has no code-driven equivalent and doesn't carry any of the navigation-drift risk.

So my position going into whatever's next: warm charcoal palette as I specified it (open to your slightly darker red for the accent, contrast-reverified), your `DeleteAction` and skeleton-loading additions folded in, one shared name for the poster component, and Claude Design scoped to two narrow, bounded prompts — a 3-frame mood check plus the icon — rather than a full screen-by-screen handoff that risks contradicting the state machine we've already locked.

**Coordinator (Claude) — decision after round 2**

CONSENSUS: YES

Here's what happened this round, and it's a cleaner convergence than it looks at first glance. Codex's round-2 message fully adopted Claude's warm-charcoal palette wholesale — background `#15100F`/`#1E1918`/`#272020`, border `#33292A`, accent `#E22C3C`, text `#F6F1ED`/`#AFA7A2` — down to the exact hex values, plus added a `textDisabled` token. Claude's round-2 message was written concurrently and doesn't know that happened yet — it's still arguing against Codex's *round-1* cooler palette (`#111217` etc.) using the "warm charcoal was already locked two phases ago" argument. But since Codex already landed on Claude's exact numbers, that fight is over before it started; there's no real daylight left on color.

On navigation, Codex explicitly took Claude's single-`ActiveInsertionFlow`-cover rule as a hard requirement — Compare and Placed are one `.fullScreenCover(item:)`, never two independent booleans — so that's locked without dispute. Component naming converged too: `PosterTile` wins over `PosterCard`, Codex's `DeleteAction` (swipe + detail-screen fallback) and skeleton-loading state both get folded in as agreed improvements.

The only place they're not saying identical words is Claude Design scope, and even that's more a difference of generosity than substance: Codex's actual resolution is icon-only, and only included the full screen-by-screen prompt as a "here it is if you still want it" fallback rather than a real ask. Claude's counter-offer — icon prompt plus one narrow, bounded 3-frame "mood check" (Library, Compare, Placed) generated strictly from the locked tokens/layout and explicitly forbidden from inventing new navigation or copy — doesn't contradict Codex's position, it's just a slightly larger version of the same "external design shouldn't become a second source of truth" stance Codex already agreed with. Nothing here needs another round.

## Final Output

**Screen-by-screen specification**

- **Library** (root, single `NavigationStack`, no tab bar): Movies|TV segmented control at top to make the track boundary spatial; genre/decade `FilterChip` row appears only once there are enough titles to make filtering meaningful (suppressed below that threshold, not shown empty); ranked list of `LibraryRow`/`PosterTile` (poster, condensed rank badge, title, year), TV rows rendered as `LockedTVRow` (dimmed tile, lock glyph, "Unlock with FaveOff+" in place of rank — never blocks add or logging); toolbar "Add Title" and "Taste Card" actions; `InlineBanner` styled two ways — `resumeInsertion` (top of list, only when a crash-recovered in-flight insertion exists, "Finish placing [Title]" → jumps straight into the active flow at persisted bounds) and `capReached` (bottom of list, only at exactly 25 ranked movies, non-blocking). States: `loading` (row skeleton/shimmer), `emptyNoItems` ("Add your first movie" doubling as CTA), `list`, `listWithFilters`, `emptyFiltered` (nudge, not blank), `loadingFailure` (inline retry).

- **Add Title**: medium-detent form sheet. Title field autofocused, Movie/TV type segmented control directly under it, year/genre visually secondary and clearly optional. Save is instant/local-first, routes to the active insertion flow (Compare) or straight to `placed` for 0/1-item cold starts. States: `idle` → `saving` → `saved`/`failed` (typed content preserved on failure, inline retry, no dropped input).

- **Compare + Placed** (one continuous ritual, one `.fullScreenCover(item:)` bound to a single `ActiveInsertionFlow` value — never two independent covers, so there is no dismiss-flash back to Library between decision and placement): full-screen, zero parent chrome. Compare shows two `PosterTile`s (poster or deterministic initials/color-block, identical visual weight either way) with a VS `CompareDivider`, tap either to choose, one secondary `SkipButton` below — no progress bar, no comparison counter, no confidence meter anywhere on this screen; "tie" is not a separate action, it's Skip. If the opposing title is deleted mid-screen: cancel gracefully back to Library with a toast ("Title removed, comparison canceled"), no crash. Placed follows immediately inside the same cover: rank reveal in condensed display type, `#N, ahead of X, behind Y` for normal placements, the locked cold-start copy ("First one in — add another to build your ranking") and skip-fallback copy ("Added near the bottom for now — compare it later to refine it") for edge cases, one button ("Keep going") back to Library. Flow states: `comparing`, `processingChoice`, `skip`, `terminatedBySkips`, `cancelledByInterruption`, `placed` (variants: `exact`, `fallback`, `coldStart`).

- **Taste Card**: large-detent sheet off the Library toolbar. Enforces a `resolvingArt` loading state before the live preview — the render never fires on partially-loaded poster art, since `ImageRenderer` snapshots synchronously and would otherwise silently capture blank tiles; trivially satisfied today since default art is synchronous initials/color-block, but the gate stays in place so it doesn't regress when optional metadata enrichment is wired in later. States: `resolvingArt`, `ready` (live preview + `ShareLink`), `renderFailed` (retry), `sharing`.

- **Paywall**: single shared sheet instance regardless of entry point (TV-lock row, cap banner, toolbar upgrade). States: `available`, `loadingProducts`, `purchasePending`, `purchaseFailed` (dismiss always reachable), `restored`, `entitled`, `dismissed`. Ranking loop is fully unaffected by any state here, success or failure.

**Design tokens**
- `backgroundCanvas` `#15100F`, `backgroundSurface` `#1E1918`, `backgroundElevated` `#272020`, `borderLine` `#33292A` (hairline borders instead of shadows — near-black fields don't render shadows well)
- `accentRed` `#E22C3C` — action fills and short large/bold display strings only, never body text (re-verify large-bold ≥3:1 against `backgroundCanvas` before final)
- `textPrimary` `#F6F1ED`, `textSecondary` `#AFA7A2` (both independently confirmed ≥4.5:1 body-weight against the canvas), `textDisabled` `#6F6660`
- Type: system font throughout for Dynamic Type integrity; condensed width (`Font.system(...).width(.condensed)`) reserved strictly for short non-wrapping strings — rank numbers, Placed hero title, section headers, chip labels. No embedded custom font file.
- Spacing: 8pt base grid (4/8/12/16/24/32/48). Radius: 16pt cards, 14pt buttons, fully-rounded chips.
- Motion: standard state changes 0.25s ease-in-out; the one cinematic beat is the Compare→Placed internal wipe inside the shared cover, ~0.35s horizontal masked transition with a faint sprocket-edge detail, collapsing to a flat 0.2s crossfade under Reduce Motion — no partial-motion exceptions.

**Component inventory**: `NavigationShell`, `LibraryRow`/`PosterTile`, `RankBadge`, `LockedTVRow`, `TrackSegmentedControl`, `FilterChip`, `InlineBanner` (resume + cap variants), `EmptyState`, `ErrorState`, `AddTitleFieldRow`, `CompareDivider`, `SkipButton`, `PlacedReveal`, `TasteCardGrid`, `PaywallProductTile`, `PrimaryButton`/`SecondaryButton`, `DeleteAction` (swipe + detail-screen fallback), `SectionSpacer`.

**Navigation/state architecture (binding rule)**: Library owns exactly one `activeSheet: SheetKind?` (Add Title / Taste Card / Paywall, each `.sheet(item:)`) and one `activeFlow: ActiveInsertionFlow?` (Compare/Placed, `.fullScreenCover(item:)`) — never competing presentations. The resume banner constructs the identical `ActiveInsertionFlow` value from persisted `lo`/`hi`/`lastComparedIndex`/`skipBudget`, so there's exactly one code path into the ritual whether triggered by a fresh Add or a crash-recovery resume. All list-driving views read through the repository/view-model boundary — no view that also does navigation touches `@Query` directly.

**Accessibility**: Compare and Placed are hard gates — measured ≥4.5:1 body / ≥3:1 large-bold contrast across the full Dynamic Type range before either is done. VoiceOver labels state the actual choice ("Select [Title A] over [Title B]"), Skip gets its own explicit label, 44×44pt minimum on every tap target including full poster bounds, no color-only status cues (lock/cap states pair a glyph with text), and the Placed VO announcement is one sentence covering rank plus both neighbors.

**Claude Design decision**: No external design pass for the app's screens — the token system, component inventory, and state model above are specific enough for direct SwiftUI implementation, and handing the full UI to an external pass risks producing frames that contradict the locked interaction contract (the single-cover invariant, the "no unprompted policy screens" rule, the honest placement copy variants) since an external tool has no visibility into that state machine. Two narrow, bounded external prompts are approved instead:
1. **App icon**: "Design an app icon for FaveOff, a cinematic movie/TV ranking app — near-black warm charcoal base (#15100F), marquee red accent (#E22C3C), bold condensed numeral or ticket/film-reel motif, award-show/red-carpet feeling, must read clearly at 60×60pt down to the smallest home-screen size, no text."
2. **Optional 3-frame mood check** (Library with a few ranked rows, Compare mid-decision, Placed reward state) — generated strictly from the token values and layout rules above, explicitly framed as a visual sanity check only, not a spec, and explicitly instructed not to invent new navigation, copy, or states.

**Quality Gate (Claude) — after round 2**

The two real disagreements the round-1 coordinator flagged are both resolved with actual reasoning, not smoothed over: the palette conflict resolves because Codex's round-2 message independently converged on Claude's exact warm-charcoal hex values (traceable back to the "warm charcoal, not true black" language locked in `design_discussion`), and the Claude Design scope question resolves because both sides' actual positions (icon-only vs. icon-plus-bounded-mood-check) turn out to be the same "external design must not become a second source of truth" stance at slightly different generosity, not a substantive fight. ## Final Output

CONSENSUS: YES

Here's what happened this round, and it's a cleaner convergence than it looks at first glance. Codex's round-2 message fully adopted Claude's warm-charcoal palette wholesale — background `#15100F`/`#1E1918`/`#272020`, border `#33292A`, accent `#E22C3C`, text `#F6F1ED`/`#AFA7A2` — down to the exact hex values, plus added a `textDisabled` token. Claude's round-2 message was written concurrently and doesn't know that happened yet — it's still arguing against Codex's *round-1* cooler palette (`#111217` etc.) using the "warm charcoal was already locked two phases ago" argument. But since Codex already landed on Claude's exact numbers, that fight is over before it started; there's no real daylight left on color.

On navigation, Codex explicitly took Claude's single-`ActiveInsertionFlow`-cover rule as a hard requirement — Compare and Placed are one `.fullScreenCover(item:)`, never two independent booleans — so that's locked without dispute. Component naming converged too: `PosterTile` wins over `PosterCard`, Codex's `DeleteAction` (swipe + detail-screen fallback) and skeleton-loading state both get folded in as agreed improvements.

The only place they're not saying identical words is Claude Design scope, and even that's more a difference of generosity than substance: Codex's actual resolution is icon-only, and only included the full screen-by-screen prompt as a "here it is if you still want it" fallback rather than a real ask. Claude's counter-offer — icon prompt plus one narrow, bounded 3-frame "mood check" (Library, Compare, Placed) generated strictly from the locked tokens/layout and explicitly forbidden from inventing new navigation or copy — doesn't contradict Codex's position, it's just a slightly larger version of the same "external design shouldn't become a second source of truth" stance Codex already agreed with. Nothing here needs another round.

IOS ARCHITECTURE REVIEW (IOS_ARCHITECTURE_REVIEW)
